rows.text: All that we are is the result of what we have thought: it is founded on our thoughts, it is made up of our thoughts. If a man speaks or acts with an evil thought, pain follows him, as the wheel follows the foot of the ox that draws the carriage.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Our lives are built from our thoughts. When a person thinks in a destructive way and then speaks or acts on it, suffering trails behind them like a cart's wheel behind the ox.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen self-blame and hopelessness in someone already feeling that their suffering is their own fault, particularly those struggling with shame, despair, or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: All that we are is the result of what we have thought: it is founded on our thoughts, it is made up of our thoughts. If a man speaks or acts with a pure thought, happiness follows him, like a shadow that never leaves him.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Our lives are shaped by our thoughts; everything we are comes from them. When someone speaks or acts with a pure mind, happiness stays with them constantly.

rows.text: "He abused me, he beat me, he defeated me, he robbed me,"—in those who harbour such thoughts hatred will never cease.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When people dwell on thoughts of being mistreated—abused, beaten, defeated, or robbed—their hatred continues without end.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel invalidating to those who have experienced abuse or harm, as it could be interpreted as blaming them for their own hatred or discouraging rightful anger.

rows.text: "He abused me, he beat me, he defeated me, he robbed me,"—in those who do not harbour such thoughts hatred will cease.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: If someone refuses to replay grievances like being insulted, harmed, defeated, or robbed, their hatred will naturally subside.
rows.riskNotes: For someone who has just disclosed abuse or is processing relational harm, this verse may feel dismissive of their pain and pressure them to suppress valid feelings of anger or hurt.

rows.text: For hatred does not cease by hatred at any time: hatred ceases by love, this is an old rule.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Hatred never ends through more hatred; it only ends through love. This is a timeless truth.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel invalidating or pressuring to someone who is angry at an aggressor or has been harmed by another, as it could be interpreted as urging forgiveness or love toward an abuser.

rows.text: The world does not know that we must all come to an end here;—but those who know it, their quarrels cease at once.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Most people don't realize that everyone will die, but those who understand this stop fighting with each other.
rows.riskNotes: The direct mention of death and coming to an end may intensify pain for someone in fresh grief.

rows.text: He who lives looking for pleasures only, his senses uncontrolled, immoderate in his food, idle, and weak, Mara (the tempter) will certainly overthrow him, as the wind throws down a weak tree.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who lives solely for pleasure, with uncontrolled senses, overindulgence in food, idleness, and weakness will be easily toppled by temptation, just as the wind knocks over a weak tree.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen self-blame and hopelessness in someone already feeling weak or at fault, as it frames suffering as a consequence of personal failing.

rows.text: He who lives without looking for pleasures, his senses well controlled, moderate in his food, faithful and strong, him Mara will certainly not overthrow, any more than the wind throws down a rocky mountain.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who does not chase after pleasures, keeps their senses in check, eats moderately, and has faith and inner strength cannot be shaken by temptation, just as a strong mountain cannot be moved by the wind.

rows.text: He who wishes to put on the yellow dress without having cleansed himself from sin, who disregards temperance and truth, is unworthy of the yellow dress.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who wants the outward status of a monk but hasn't done the inner work of purifying their own wrongdoing, and who ignores self-discipline and honesty, doesn't truly deserve that role.
rows.riskNotes: The blunt judgment of unworthiness may intensify shame and self-condemnation in someone already feeling sinful or worthless, potentially deepening despair or suicidal ideation.

rows.text: But he who has cleansed himself from sin, is well grounded in all virtues, and regards also temperance and truth, he is indeed worthy of the yellow dress.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has removed their moral stains, is firmly established in good qualities, and values moderation and honesty is truly fit to wear the monastic robe.

rows.text: They who imagine truth in untruth, and see untruth in truth, never arrive at truth, but follow vain desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who mistake falsehood for truth and truth for falsehood never find what is true; instead, they chase empty desires.

rows.text: They who know truth in truth, and untruth in untruth, arrive at truth, and follow true desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Those who accurately distinguish what is true from what is false come to understand the truth and naturally pursue wholesome aims.

rows.text: As rain breaks through an ill-thatched house, passion will break through an unreflecting mind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as rain leaks through a poorly built roof, strong desires will overwhelm a mind that does not reflect on its thoughts.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may be heard as blaming the listener for their overwhelming emotions, which could deepen shame or self-blame in someone already struggling with those feelings.

rows.text: As rain does not break through a well-thatched house, passion will not break through a well-reflecting mind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A mind that is well-guarded and reflective prevents craving from taking hold, just as a well-thatched roof keeps out the rain.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may be harmful to someone struggling with shame or self-blame, as it implies that their inability to control passion reflects a poorly disciplined mind.

rows.text: The evil-doer mourns in this world, and he mourns in the next; he mourns in both. He mourns and suffers when he sees the evil of his own work.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who does wrong experiences sorrow both in this life and the next, grieving when they recognize the harm they have caused.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of shame and hopelessness in someone already blaming themselves or feeling despair, as it emphasizes the inescapable suffering of the 'evil-doer'.

rows.text: The virtuous man delights in this world, and he delights in the next; he delights in both. He delights and rejoices, when he sees the purity of his own work.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who acts with integrity finds happiness in this life and the next. They feel joy when they recognize the goodness of their own actions.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of inadequacy for someone already struggling with shame or self-blame, as it ties delight to the perception of one's own purity.

rows.text: The evil-doer suffers in this world, and he suffers in the next; he suffers in both. He suffers when he thinks of the evil he has done; he suffers more when going on the evil path.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who does wrong experiences suffering both in this life and the next; they suffer when reflecting on their misdeeds and even more as they continue down that harmful path.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may intensify feelings of shame and hopelessness in someone who already blames themselves or sees themselves as irredeemably evil.

rows.text: The virtuous man is happy in this world, and he is happy in the next; he is happy in both. He is happy when he thinks of the good he has done; he is still more happy when going on the good path.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who lives ethically feels happiness in this life and beyond. Reflecting on their own good actions brings joy, and continuing on a wholesome path brings even deeper happiness.

rows.text: The thoughtless man, even if he can recite a large portion (of the law), but is not a doer of it, has no share in the priesthood, but is like a cowherd counting the cows of others.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who can recite many teachings but does not put them into practice is like a cowherd counting another's cows—they get no real benefit.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could deepen feelings of worthlessness in someone already struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair, as it condemns those who do not practice what they preach.

rows.text: The follower of the law, even if he can recite only a small portion (of the law), but, having forsaken passion and hatred and foolishness, possesses true knowledge and serenity of mind, he, caring for nothing in this world or that to come, has indeed a share in the priesthood.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who truly follows the teachings, even if they know only a little, but has let go of craving, anger, and ignorance, and has gained insight and inner calm, is free from worldly concerns and attains spiritual fulfillment.
rows.riskNotes: The phrase 'caring for nothing in this world or that to come' could be misinterpreted by someone in despair or suicidal ideation as endorsing detachment from life, potentially deepening hopelessness.

rows.text: Earnestness is the path of immortality (Nirvana), thoughtlessness the path of death. Those who are in earnest do not die, those who are thoughtless are as if dead already.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Diligence and careful attention lead to the deathless state, while negligence leads to spiritual death. The diligent are truly alive; the negligent are as good as dead.
rows.riskNotes: The stark equation of thoughtlessness with death may deepen hopelessness in someone already despairing or grieving, and the mention of death could be triggering for those with suicidal ideation.

rows.text: Those who are advanced in earnestness, having understood this clearly, delight in earnestness, and rejoice in the knowledge of the Ariyas (the elect).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who are deeply committed to being mindful, having clearly seen its importance, find joy in that mindfulness and take delight in the understanding that comes from wise individuals.

rows.text: These wise people, meditative, steady, always possessed of strong powers, attain to Nirvana, the highest happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Those who are wise, practice meditation, remain steady, and develop strong inner qualities reach a state of complete peace and happiness.

rows.text: If an earnest person has roused himself, if he is not forgetful, if his deeds are pure, if he acts with consideration, if he restrains himself, and lives according to law,—then his glory will increase.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When someone is diligent, attentive, acts with integrity, thinks before acting, exercises restraint, and lives by moral principles, their reputation and well-being increase.

rows.text: By rousing himself, by earnestness, by restraint and control, the wise man may make for himself an island which no flood can overwhelm.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who motivates themselves, stays diligent, and practices self-discipline can build an inner refuge that no overwhelming force can destroy.

rows.text: Fools follow after vanity, men of evil wisdom. The wise man keeps earnestness as his best jewel.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who lack wisdom chase after empty distractions, while a wise person treasures a careful, diligent attitude as their most valuable possession.

rows.text: Follow not after vanity, nor after the enjoyment of love and lust! He who is earnest and meditative, obtains ample joy.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Do not chase after superficial pleasures or sensual desires. A person who is diligent and reflective finds deep happiness.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may intensify shame or self-blame in someone already struggling with guilt over desires or past relationships, and could feel invalidating to those disclosing abuse.

rows.text: When the learned man drives away vanity by earnestness, he, the wise, climbing the terraced heights of wisdom, looks down upon the fools, serene he looks upon the toiling crowd, as one that stands on a mountain looks down upon them that stand upon the plain.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A learned person, through earnest effort, overcomes vanity and attains wisdom, then looks down on the foolish with serene detachment, like a mountain-dweller viewing those on the plain.

rows.text: Earnest among the thoughtless, awake among the sleepers, the wise man advances like a racer, leaving behind the hack.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who stays diligent and aware while others are careless and inattentive makes rapid progress, like a swift horse leaving a slow one behind.

rows.text: By earnestness did Maghavan (Indra) rise to the lordship of the gods. People praise earnestness; thoughtlessness is always blamed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Maghavan (Indra) reached the highest position among the gods by being consistently diligent. People admire diligence, while carelessness is always condemned.

rows.text: A Bhikshu (mendicant) who delights in earnestness, who looks with fear on thoughtlessness, moves about like fire, burning all his fetters, small or large.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A monk who enjoys being attentive and is wary of negligence destroys all forms of bondage, just as a fire burns up whatever it touches.

rows.text: A Bhikshu (mendicant) who delights in reflection, who looks with fear on thoughtlessness, cannot fall away (from his perfect state)—he is close upon Nirvana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A monk who enjoys careful attention and is wary of carelessness stays on the path and is near liberation.

rows.text: As a fletcher makes straight his arrow, a wise man makes straight his trembling and unsteady thought, which is difficult to guard, difficult to hold back.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A wise person trains their restless, shaky mind to become steady, just as an arrow-maker carefully straightens an arrow. The mind is hard to watch over and restrain.

rows.text: As a fish taken from his watery home and thrown on dry ground, our thought trembles all over in order to escape the dominion of Mara (the tempter).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Our mind flails and struggles like a fish out of water, desperately trying to break free from the grip of craving and inner turmoil.
rows.riskNotes: The vivid simile of a fish struggling out of water may intensify feelings of panic, despair, or grief in those already overwhelmed.

rows.text: It is good to tame the mind, which is difficult to hold in and flighty, rushing wherever it listeth; a tamed mind brings happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The mind is restless and hard to control, but training it leads to well-being.

rows.text: Let the wise man guard his thoughts, for they are difficult to perceive, very artful, and they rush wherever they list: thoughts well guarded bring happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A wise person keeps a close watch on their thoughts because thoughts are subtle, tricky, and wander freely. When thoughts are carefully guarded, they lead to happiness.

rows.text: Those who bridle their mind which travels far, moves about alone, is without a body, and hides in the chamber (of the heart), will be free from the bonds of Mara (the tempter).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The mind is restless, solitary, invisible, and hidden within. Learning to restrain it frees a person from inner bondage.

rows.text: If a man's thoughts are unsteady, if he does not know the true law, if his peace of mind is troubled, his knowledge will never be perfect.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person's thinking is unstable, they don't understand the true principles, and their inner calm is disturbed, their understanding remains incomplete.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of inadequacy and hopelessness in someone already struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair by implying that their troubled mind permanently bars them from true understanding.

rows.text: If a man's thoughts are not dissipated, if his mind is not perplexed, if he has ceased to think of good or evil, then there is no fear for him while he is watchful.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person's thoughts are not scattered, their mind is clear, and they no longer dwell on good or bad, they have nothing to fear as long as they remain alert.

rows.text: Knowing that this body is (fragile) like a jar, and making this thought firm like a fortress, one should attack Mara (the tempter) with the weapon of knowledge, one should watch him when conquered, and should never rest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The body is as breakable as a clay jar. Make your mind as solid as a fortress, use insight to overcome destructive impulses, and stay watchful without ever relaxing.

rows.text: Before long, alas! this body will lie on the earth, despised, without understanding, like a useless log.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Soon enough, this body will be dead and discarded, lying on the ground like a worthless log, devoid of any awareness.
rows.riskNotes: The stark image of a lifeless, despised body could intensify despair or suicidal thoughts, and may deeply wound someone in fresh grief.

rows.text: Whatever a hater may do to a hater, or an enemy to an enemy, a wrongly-directed mind will do us greater mischief.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A mind that is not properly directed can cause more harm to oneself than any enemy or hater could.

rows.text: Not a mother, not a father will do so much, nor any other relative; a well-directed mind will do us greater service.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Even the care of parents and family cannot compare to the benefit that comes from a mind that is properly guided.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel dismissive of parental love to someone in fresh grief, as it suggests a parent's service is less than that of a well-directed mind.

rows.text: Who shall overcome this earth, and the world of Yama (the lord of the departed), and the world of the gods? Who shall find out the plainly shown path of virtue, as a clever man finds out the (right) flower?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: It asks who can conquer the earthly realm, the domain of death, and the world of the gods, and who can recognize the obvious path of virtue, like an expert picking the right flower.

rows.text: The disciple will overcome the earth, and the world of Yama, and the world of the gods. The disciple will find out the plainly shown path of virtue, as a clever man finds out the (right) flower.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The disciple will conquer the earthly realm, the realm of death, and the divine realm. They will discover the clear path of moral excellence, just as a skilled person picks the correct flower.

rows.text: He who knows that this body is like froth, and has learnt that it is as unsubstantial as a mirage, will break the flower-pointed arrow of Mara, and never see the king of death.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who realizes the body is as insubstantial as foam and a mirage can overcome the seductive pull of craving and escape the cycle of death.

rows.text: Death carries off a man who is gathering flowers and whose mind is distracted, as a flood carries off a sleeping village.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is distracted by pursuing pleasures is suddenly overtaken by death, just as a sleeping village is swept away by a flood.
rows.riskNotes: The vivid image of death sweeping away a distracted person could intensify feelings of panic, despair, or grief, and may be particularly distressing for someone experiencing suicidal thoughts or recent loss.

rows.text: Death subdues a man who is gathering flowers, and whose mind is distracted, before he is satiated in his pleasures.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is distracted while chasing pleasures gets overtaken by death before they can ever feel satisfied.
rows.riskNotes: The stark image of death abruptly ending life's pleasures may intensify panic, despair, grief, or suicidal thoughts in those already struggling with mortality or loss.

rows.text: As the bee collects nectar and departs without injuring the flower, or its colour or scent, so let a sage dwell in his village.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A wise person moves through their community taking only what they need, without causing harm or disturbance, just as a bee takes nectar without damaging the flower.

rows.text: Not the perversities of others, not their sins of commission or omission, but his own misdeeds and negligences should a sage take notice of.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A wise person focuses on their own mistakes and carelessness, not on the faults of others.
rows.riskNotes: For someone already burdened by shame or self-blame, this passage may intensify self-criticism; for those harmed by others, it could feel like their pain is being dismissed.

rows.text: Like a beautiful flower, full of colour, but without scent, are the fine but fruitless words of him who does not act accordingly.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Words, no matter how beautiful, are worthless if they aren't accompanied by deeds. They're like a colorful flower with no scent.

rows.text: But, like a beautiful flower, full of colour and full of scent, are the fine and fruitful words of him who acts accordingly.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person's actions align with their words, their speech becomes as beautiful and beneficial as a vibrant, fragrant flower.

rows.text: As many kinds of wreaths can be made from a heap of flowers, so many good things may be achieved by a mortal when once he is born.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person can accomplish many good deeds in life, just as many different garlands can be fashioned from a pile of flowers.

rows.text: The scent of flowers does not travel against the wind, nor (that of) sandal-wood, or of Tagara and Mallika flowers; but the odour of good people travels even against the wind; a good man pervades every place.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Unlike the scent of flowers, which cannot travel against the wind, the influence of a good person reaches all directions, overcoming any obstacles.

rows.text: Sandal-wood or Tagara, a lotus-flower, or a Vassiki, among these sorts of perfumes, the perfume of virtue is unsurpassed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The scent of sandalwood, tagara, lotus, or vassiki is pleasant, but the quality of a good person's virtue is far superior.

rows.text: Mean is the scent that comes from Tagara and sandal-wood;—the perfume of those who possess virtue rises up to the gods as the highest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The scent of sandalwood and other perfumes is ordinary; the goodness of a virtuous person spreads far and is recognized as supreme, even by the gods.

rows.text: Of the people who possess these virtues, who live without thoughtlessness, and who are emancipated through true knowledge, Mara, the tempter, never finds the way.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Those who are morally upright, consistently attentive, and freed through genuine insight are beyond the reach of any inner destructive force.

rows.text: As on a heap of rubbish cast upon the highway the lily will grow full of sweet perfume and delight, thus the disciple of the truly enlightened Buddha shines forth by his knowledge among those who are like rubbish, among the people that walk in darkness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Even in a degraded or ignorant environment, a person of wisdom can flourish and be a source of goodness, just as a lily grows fragrant on a rubbish heap.
rows.riskNotes: The simile of people as 'rubbish' walking in darkness may deepen feelings of worthlessness and hopelessness in those already struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair.

rows.text: Long is the night to him who is awake; long is a mile to him who is tired; long is life to the foolish who do not know the true law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A sleepless person feels the night drag on; a weary traveler feels the road stretch endlessly. Similarly, people who lack understanding of the true way of things experience life as a long, tiresome ordeal.
rows.riskNotes: A person already feeling shame or self-blame may interpret this as confirmation that their suffering is their own fault for being foolish.

rows.text: If a traveller does not meet with one who is his better, or his equal, let him firmly keep to his solitary journey; there is no companionship with a fool.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: If you cannot find a companion who is your equal or superior, it is better to continue alone; there is no benefit in associating with a fool.

rows.text: "These sons belong to me, and this wealth belongs to me," with such thoughts a fool is tormented. He himself does not belong to himself; how much less sons and wealth?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Thinking 'my children, my wealth' torments a person. They don't even own themselves, so how could they own others?
rows.riskNotes: This passage may wound those grieving a loss by dismissing the significance of loved ones, and may deepen shame or self-blame in those already feeling foolish for their attachments.

rows.text: The fool who knows his foolishness, is wise at least so far. But a fool who thinks himself wise, he is called a fool indeed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who recognizes their own lack of understanding has at least that much insight. But someone who is actually uninformed yet believes they are knowledgeable is truly foolish.

rows.text: If a fool be associated with a wise man even all his life, he will perceive the truth as little as a spoon perceives the taste of soup.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A foolish person can spend their whole life in the company of a wise one and still not understand the truth, much like a spoon never tastes the food it stirs.
rows.riskNotes: The label 'fool' and the assertion of imperceptiveness could deepen feelings of worthlessness or hopelessness in someone already experiencing shame, self-blame, or despair.

rows.text: If an intelligent man be associated for one minute only with a wise man, he will soon perceive the truth, as the tongue perceives the taste of soup.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Even a short time spent with a wise person can help someone who is perceptive quickly understand what is true, just as the tongue immediately tastes soup.

rows.text: Fools of little understanding have themselves for their greatest enemies, for they do evil deeds which must bear bitter fruits.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who lack insight harm themselves through their own actions, because harmful deeds inevitably lead to painful results.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may reinforce feelings of self-blame and shame in someone already struggling with guilt, as it attributes suffering to one's own foolish actions.

rows.text: That deed is not well done of which a man must repent, and the reward of which he receives crying and with a tearful face.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: An action is not well done if it leads to regret and its results are received with tears and sorrow.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of guilt and regret in someone already experiencing shame or self-blame, as it emphasizes the sorrow that follows unwise actions.

rows.text: No, that deed is well done of which a man does not repent, and the reward of which he receives gladly and cheerfully.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A deed is truly good when it causes no regret and its outcome is received with happiness and satisfaction.

rows.text: As long as the evil deed done does not bear fruit, the fool thinks it is like honey; but when it ripens, then the fool suffers grief.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: An evil deed may seem pleasant at first, but when its consequences eventually arrive, the doer experiences pain.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may reinforce feelings of self-blame and shame in someone already struggling with guilt or despair, as it suggests that suffering is a direct result of one's own wrongdoing.

rows.text: Let a fool month after month eat his food (like an ascetic) with the tip of a blade of Kusa grass, yet he is not worth the sixteenth particle of those who have well weighed the law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Even if a person practices extreme self-denial for a long time, they are still far less valuable than someone who has deeply understood the truth.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's dismissal of a fool's efforts as worthless could deepen feelings of shame, self-blame, or despair in someone already struggling with self-worth or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: An evil deed, like newly-drawn milk, does not turn (suddenly); smouldering, like fire covered by ashes, it follows the fool.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A harmful act doesn't show its effects immediately. It lingers quietly, like embers under ash, and eventually catches up with the person who did it.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen shame and hopelessness in someone already burdened by guilt over past actions, as it emphasizes that evil deeds inevitably follow the doer.

rows.text: And when the evil deed, after it has become known, brings sorrow to the fool, then it destroys his bright lot, nay, it cleaves his head.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a foolish person's bad action is exposed, it causes them grief and ruins their good fortune, even destroying them completely.
rows.riskNotes: The vivid imagery of destruction and head-cleaving may intensify shame, self-blame, or despair in someone already feeling guilty or hopeless, and could be triggering for those with suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: Let the fool wish for a false reputation, for precedence among the Bhikshus, for lordship in the convents, for worship among other people!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A foolish person longs for undeserved reputation, for higher status among monks, for authority in monasteries, and for admiration from others.

rows.text: "May both the layman and he who has left the world think that this is done by me; may they be subject to me in everything which is to be done or is not to be done," thus is the mind of the fool, and his desire and pride increase.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A fool wants others to acknowledge his deeds and to obey him in all matters; this mindset only makes his craving and arrogance grow.

rows.text: "One is the road that leads to wealth, another the road that leads to Nirvana;" if the Bhikshu, the disciple of Buddha, has learnt this, he will not yearn for honour, he will strive after separation from the world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: There is a path to material gain and a separate path to liberation. A follower of the Buddha who understands this will not chase after social status but will instead work toward detachment from worldly life.

rows.text: If you see an intelligent man who tells you where true treasures are to be found, who shows what is to be avoided, and administers reproofs, follow that wise man; it will be better, not worse, for those who follow him.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: If you meet someone who can point you toward genuine value, warn you about pitfalls, and correct your mistakes, stick with that person. It will only benefit you.
rows.riskNotes: The endorsement of reproof may reinforce feelings of unworthiness in someone already struggling with shame or self-blame.

rows.text: Let him admonish, let him teach, let him forbid what is improper!—he will be beloved of the good, by the bad he will be hated.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who corrects others, teaches them, and stops them from doing wrong will be liked by good people and disliked by bad people.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may pressure someone who has just disclosed abuse to confront their abuser, which could be unsafe.

rows.text: Do not have evil-doers for friends, do not have low people for friends: have virtuous people for friends, have for friends the best of men.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: One should not keep company with wrongdoers or low-minded people; instead, associate with those who are virtuous and the best among people.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may reinforce feelings of unworthiness in someone already experiencing acute shame or self-blame, as they might interpret it as labeling them 'low' or 'evil'.

rows.text: He who drinks in the law lives happily with a serene mind: the sage rejoices always in the law, as preached by the elect (Ariyas).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who deeply absorbs the teachings lives happily with a calm mind. The wise always find joy in the teachings shared by those who are noble.

rows.text: Well-makers lead the water (wherever they like); fletchers bend the arrow; carpenters bend a log of wood; wise people fashion themselves.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as engineers channel water, fletchers straighten arrows, and carpenters shape wood, a wise person actively molds their own character.

rows.text: As a solid rock is not shaken by the wind, wise people falter not amidst blame and praise.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Wise people remain steady and unaffected by criticism or compliments, just as a solid rock is not moved by the wind.
rows.riskNotes: The mention of not faltering amidst blame may intensify feelings of inadequacy in someone already struggling with shame or self-blame.

rows.text: Wise people, after they have listened to the laws, become serene, like a deep, smooth, and still lake.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When wise people hear the teachings, they become deeply calm and still, like a smooth, deep lake.

rows.text: Good people walk on whatever befall, the good do not prattle, longing for pleasure; whether touched by happiness or sorrow wise people never appear elated or depressed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Those who are truly good stay even-tempered through all circumstances. They don't talk idly about chasing pleasure, and when happiness or sorrow comes, the wise don't get carried away or downcast.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may make someone in despair, grief, or self-blame feel judged for their emotional state, as it implies that wise people do not appear depressed.

rows.text: If, whether for his own sake, or for the sake of others, a man wishes neither for a son, nor for wealth, nor for lordship, and if he does not wish for his own success by unfair means, then he is good, wise, and virtuous.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who doesn't crave having children, money, or authority, and who won't use dishonest tactics to get ahead, is considered genuinely good, wise, and upright.

rows.text: Few are there among men who arrive at the other shore (become Arhats); the other people here run up and down the shore.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Only a small number of people reach full awakening; the vast majority just keep wandering aimlessly in ordinary life.

rows.text: But those who, when the law has been well preached to them, follow the law, will pass across the dominion of death, however difficult to overcome.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who hear a clear teaching and then actually live by it will get past death's grip, even though that's a hard thing to do.

rows.text: A wise man should leave the dark state (of ordinary life), and follow the bright state (of the Bhikshu). After going from his home to a homeless state, he should in his retirement look for enjoyment where there seemed to be no enjoyment. Leaving all pleasures behind, and calling nothing his own, the wise man should purge himself from all the troubles of the mind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A wise person leaves ordinary life to become a homeless monk, finding joy in renunciation where others see no joy. By giving up all pleasures and possessions, they cleanse their mind of all disturbances.

rows.text: Those whose mind is well grounded in the (seven) elements of knowledge, who without clinging to anything, rejoice in freedom from attachment, whose appetites have been conquered, and who are full of light, are free (even) in this world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person whose mind is deeply rooted in understanding, who lets go of all clinging, finds joy in non-attachment, has mastered their desires, and is inwardly luminous, lives freely even in this world.

rows.text: There is no suffering for him who has finished his journey, and abandoned grief, who has freed himself on all sides, and thrown off all fetters.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who has reached the end of the journey, released all grief, and become completely unbound no longer experiences suffering.

rows.text: They depart with their thoughts well-collected, they are not happy in their abode; like swans who have left their lake, they leave their house and home.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Those with well-disciplined minds find no satisfaction in staying put; they abandon their homes and belongings, like swans leaving a lake behind.

rows.text: Men who have no riches, who live on recognised food, who have perceived void and unconditioned freedom (Nirvana), their path is difficult to understand, like that of birds in the air.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who have no possessions, accept only what is freely given as food, and have directly understood the nature of emptiness and liberation live in a way that others find nearly impossible to follow or comprehend.

rows.text: He whose appetites are stilled, who is not absorbed in enjoyment, who has perceived void and unconditioned freedom (Nirvana), his path is difficult to understand, like that of birds in the air.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has quieted their desires, doesn't get caught up in pleasure, and has realized the empty and unconditioned nature of freedom—their way of living is hard to trace, like the flight of birds across the sky.

rows.text: The gods even envy him whose senses, like horses well broken in by the driver, have been subdued, who is free from pride, and free from appetites.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has trained their senses, like a skilled driver controls horses, and who has let go of pride and desire, is admired even by divine beings.

rows.text: Such a one who does his duty is tolerant like the earth, like Indra's bolt; he is like a lake without mud; no new births are in store for him.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who does what they should is as patient as the earth, as firm as a thunderbolt, as clear as a lake without mud, and will not be born again.

rows.text: His thought is quiet, quiet are his word and deed, when he has obtained freedom by true knowledge, when he has thus become a quiet man.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person has gained liberation through genuine understanding, their thinking, speaking, and acting become calm and still.

rows.text: The man who is free from credulity, but knows the uncreated, who has cut all ties, removed all temptations, renounced all desires, he is the greatest of men.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who is not gullible, who directly knows the unconditioned, has severed every bond, removed all inner temptations, and let go of all craving is the most excellent kind of person.

rows.text: In a hamlet or in a forest, in the deep water or on the dry land, wherever venerable persons (Arhanta) dwell, that place is delightful.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Wherever enlightened people live, whether in a village or a forest, on water or on land, that place becomes pleasant.

rows.text: Forests are delightful; where the world finds no delight, there the passionless will find delight, for they look not for pleasures.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who are free from craving enjoy quiet natural places that others find unappealing, because they are not chasing sensory pleasures.

rows.text: Even though a speech be a thousand (of words), but made up of senseless words, one word of sense is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A long speech full of meaningless words is less valuable than a single meaningful word that, when heard, brings calm.

rows.text: Even though a Gatha (poem) be a thousand (of words), but made up of senseless words, one word of a Gatha is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A thousand meaningless words are not as valuable as a single word that, when heard, brings a person to stillness.

rows.text: Though a man recite a hundred Gathas made up of senseless words, one word of the law is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Reciting many meaningless verses is less valuable than hearing a single true teaching that brings inner stillness.

rows.text: If one man conquer in battle a thousand times thousand men, and if another conquer himself, he is the greatest of conquerors.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Defeating many enemies in battle is a lesser achievement than mastering one's own mind and actions.

rows.text: One's own self conquered is better than all other people; not even a god, a Gandharva, not Mara with Brahman could change into defeat the victory of a man who has vanquished himself, and always lives under restraint.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Conquering yourself is more valuable than defeating others. No external force can undo the victory of someone who has mastered themselves and lives with discipline.

rows.text: If a man for a hundred years sacrifice month after month with a thousand, and if he but for one moment pay homage to a man whose soul is grounded (in true knowledge), better is that homage than sacrifice for a hundred years.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A hundred years of lavish monthly sacrifices are worth less than a single moment of respect shown to someone who has realized deep truth.

rows.text: If a man for a hundred years worship Agni (fire) in the forest, and if he but for one moment pay homage to a man whose soul is grounded (in true knowledge), better is that homage than sacrifice for a hundred years.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Worshiping fire in the forest for a hundred years is less valuable than a single moment of honoring a person who has genuine understanding.

rows.text: Whatever a man sacrifice in this world as an offering or as an oblation for a whole year in order to gain merit, the whole of it is not worth a quarter (a farthing); reverence shown to the righteous is better.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A full year of ritual offerings and sacrifices to earn merit is worth less than a tiny fraction compared to simply showing respect to a virtuous person.

rows.text: He who always greets and constantly reveres the aged, four things will increase to him, viz. life, beauty, happiness, power.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who consistently shows respect and honor to older people will experience growth in four areas: lifespan, physical attractiveness, happiness, and influence.

rows.text: But he who lives a hundred years, vicious and unrestrained, a life of one day is better if a man is virtuous and reflecting.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A long life spent without moral discipline or self-control is worth less than a single day lived with integrity and reflective awareness.
rows.riskNotes: This verse may deepen feelings of worthlessness in someone already struggling with shame or self-blame, and its preference for a short virtuous life could be dangerous for those with suicidal ideation.

rows.text: And he who lives a hundred years, ignorant and unrestrained, a life of one day is better if a man is wise and reflecting.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A single day lived with wisdom and reflection is more valuable than a hundred years of ignorance and lack of self-control.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may be harmful to individuals struggling with shame or self-blame, as it could be interpreted as devaluing a life not yet lived wisely, potentially reinforcing feelings of worthlessness or despair.

rows.text: And he who lives a hundred years, idle and weak, a life of one day is better if a man has attained firm strength.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A long life spent in idleness and weakness is worthless compared to even a single day lived with resolute strength.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could deepen feelings of worthlessness in someone already struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair by implying that a life without strength is wasted.

rows.text: And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing beginning and end, a life of one day is better if a man sees beginning and end.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A single day lived with insight into the arising and passing of phenomena is more valuable than a hundred years without such understanding.
rows.riskNotes: The comparison may be distressing to those grieving a loss or feeling that their life lacks meaning, as it could be read as devaluing a long life without spiritual insight.

rows.text: And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing the immortal place, a life of one day is better if a man sees the immortal place.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A life of a hundred years without realizing the deathless state is worth less than a single day lived with that direct insight.
rows.riskNotes: The comparison of a long life without insight to a single day with insight could be misinterpreted by someone in despair or with suicidal thoughts as endorsing a short life, potentially reinforcing hopelessness or harmful impulses.

rows.text: And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing the highest law, a life of one day is better if a man sees the highest law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A single day lived with genuine insight into the ultimate truth is more valuable than a hundred years without such understanding.

rows.text: If a man would hasten towards the good, he should keep his thought away from evil; if a man does what is good slothfully, his mind delights in evil.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: To make quick progress in goodness, one must guard one's thoughts from evil. If good deeds are done lazily, the mind still takes pleasure in harmful things.

rows.text: If a man commits a sin, let him not do it again; let him not delight in sin: pain is the outcome of evil.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person does something harmful, they should avoid repeating it and not find enjoyment in it, since harmful actions bring pain.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may intensify feelings of guilt and self-condemnation in someone already struggling with shame or self-blame, as it directly links personal suffering to one's own wrongdoing.

rows.text: If a man does what is good, let him do it again; let him delight in it: happiness is the outcome of good.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Doing good repeatedly and taking pleasure in it brings happiness as a result.

rows.text: Even an evil-doer sees happiness as long as his evil deed has not ripened; but when his evil deed has ripened, then does the evil-doer see evil.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who does wrong may feel happy for a while, but once the consequences catch up, they will suffer.

rows.text: Even a good man sees evil days, as long as his good deed has not ripened; but when his good deed has ripened, then does the good man see happy days.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who does good may still face hardship until the positive results of their actions come to fruition; once those results mature, they experience happiness.
rows.riskNotes: A person in self-blame or shame may interpret the mention of 'evil days' as confirmation that their suffering is their own fault, deepening their distress.

rows.text: Let no man think lightly of evil, saying in his heart, It will not come nigh unto me. Even by the falling of water-drops a water-pot is filled; the fool becomes full of evil, even if he gather it little by little.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Small harmful actions add up over time, just like dripping water fills a container. A person who dismisses minor misdeeds as harmless will eventually be weighed down by them.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of self-condemnation in someone already struggling with shame or self-blame, as it labels the one who accumulates evil a fool.

rows.text: Let no man think lightly of good, saying in his heart, It will not come nigh unto me. Even by the falling of water-drops a water-pot is filled; the wise man becomes full of good, even if he gather it little by little.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Don't dismiss small good actions as insignificant. Just as dripping water eventually fills a pot, a wise person accumulates goodness gradually through consistent small efforts.

rows.text: Let a man avoid evil deeds, as a merchant, if he has few companions and carries much wealth, avoids a dangerous road; as a man who loves life avoids poison.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should steer clear of harmful actions, just as a merchant carrying valuable goods alone avoids a risky path, or as someone who values life stays away from poison.

rows.text: He who has no wound on his hand, may touch poison with his hand; poison does not affect one who has no wound; nor is there evil for one who does not commit evil.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as poison only harms someone with an open wound, evil only affects those who actually do wrong. If you don't do evil, evil won't touch you.
