rows.riskNotes: This passage may lead someone already feeling shame or self-blame to interpret their suffering as proof of their own wrongdoing, deepening despair.

rows.text: If a man offend a harmless, pure, and innocent person, the evil falls back upon that fool, like light dust thrown up against the wind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When someone harms a person who is gentle and blameless, the harm they intended comes back to them, just as fine dust thrown into the wind blows back onto the thrower.

rows.text: Some people are born again; evil-doers go to hell; righteous people go to heaven; those who are free from all worldly desires attain Nirvana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Actions have consequences: harmful behavior leads to suffering, good behavior leads to reward, but only by completely letting go of all desires can one find ultimate freedom.
rows.riskNotes: The mention of hell for evil-doers may deepen self-condemnation in someone already struggling with shame or self-blame.

rows.text: Not in the sky, not in the midst of the sea, not if we enter into the clefts of the mountains, is there known a spot in the whole world where a man might be freed from an evil deed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: No place in the world can shield a person from the results of their harmful actions.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of inescapable guilt for someone already struggling with shame or self-blame, potentially worsening despair or suicidal ideation.

rows.text: Not in the sky, not in the midst of the sea, not if we enter into the clefts of the mountains, is there known a spot in the whole world where death could not overcome (the mortal).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: There is no place in the world—not the sky, the ocean, or mountain caves—where a person can hide from death.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen pain for someone in fresh grief by underscoring that death is unavoidable.

rows.text: All men tremble at punishment, all men fear death; remember that you are like unto them, and do not kill, nor cause slaughter.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Nobody wants to be punished or to die. Because you're no different from anyone else in this, you shouldn't kill or cause others to be killed.
rows.riskNotes: The call to see oneself in the aggressor may feel invalidating to someone who has been harmed by another, potentially deepening rage or pain.

rows.text: All men tremble at punishment, all men love life; remember that thou art like unto them, and do not kill, nor cause slaughter.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Everyone fears harm and cherishes their life. Recognize your own similarity to others and refrain from killing or causing others to kill.
rows.riskNotes: The direct command against killing may feel dismissive or suppressive to someone experiencing rage at an aggressor or healing from relational harm, as it could be interpreted as forbidding self-protective anger.

rows.text: He who seeking his own happiness punishes or kills beings who also long for happiness, will not find happiness after death.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who harms or kills other beings to secure their own happiness will not find happiness after death.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may intensify feelings of shame and self-blame in someone who has caused harm, by suggesting they will not find happiness after death.

rows.text: He who seeking his own happiness does not punish or kill beings who also long for happiness, will find happiness after death.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who wants to be happy and refrains from harming or killing others, who also want happiness, will experience happiness after death.

rows.text: Do not speak harshly to anybody; those who are spoken to will answer thee in the same way. Angry speech is painful, blows for blows will touch thee.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Harsh words provoke others to respond harshly, and angry speech brings painful consequences.

rows.text: If, like a shattered metal plate (gong), thou utter not, then thou hast reached Nirvana; contention is not known to thee.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Staying silent, like a cracked gong that cannot ring, leads to inner peace and freedom from arguments.
rows.riskNotes: The instruction to be silent and avoid contention may feel invalidating or oppressive to someone who needs to speak out against harm or injustice.

rows.text: As a cowherd with his staff drives his cows into the stable, so do Age and Death drive the life of men.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as a cowherd uses a stick to guide cattle into their pen, aging and death steer human life toward its inevitable end.
rows.riskNotes: The vivid simile of death driving life may deepen feelings of helplessness and sorrow for someone in fresh grief.

rows.text: A fool does not know when he commits his evil deeds: but the wicked man burns by his own deeds, as if burnt by fire.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A fool is unaware of the harm they cause, but the person who knowingly does wrong suffers from their own actions, like being burned by fire.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may intensify feelings of self-blame and hopelessness in someone already struggling with shame or despair, as it frames suffering as a direct consequence of one's own wrongdoing.

rows.text: He who inflicts pain on innocent and harmless persons, will soon come to one of these ten states:
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who harms those who are innocent and pose no threat will quickly experience one of ten severe consequences.
rows.riskNotes: This warning of impending suffering for harming innocents may deepen self-blame and shame in someone already feeling guilty about past actions.

rows.text: He will have cruel suffering, loss, injury of the body, heavy affliction, or loss of mind,
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Wrong actions lead to severe suffering, loss, bodily harm, heavy distress, or mental breakdown.
rows.riskNotes: This passage vividly threatens cruel suffering, bodily injury, and loss of mind, which can intensify despair, panic, or self-blame in someone already fragile.

rows.text: Or a misfortune coming from the king, or a fearful accusation, or loss of relations, or destruction of treasures,
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person may face trouble from authorities, a frightening accusation, the loss of family or friends, or the ruin of their wealth.
rows.riskNotes: Listing misfortunes such as loss of loved ones and destruction of treasures may deepen despair and grief for those already in crisis.

rows.text: Or lightning-fire will burn his houses; and when his body is destroyed, the fool will go to hell.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A fool may lose his home to a sudden fire, and after death he will face torment in hell.
rows.riskNotes: This passage threatens violent destruction and hell, which could deepen feelings of worthlessness, fear, or hopelessness in someone already struggling with shame, grief, or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: Not nakedness, not platted hair, not dirt, not fasting, or lying on the earth, not rubbing with dust, not sitting motionless, can purify a mortal who has not overcome desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: External practices like going naked, wearing matted hair, living in dirt, fasting, lying on the ground, rubbing the body with dust, or sitting perfectly still cannot cleanse a person who has not conquered their inner cravings.

rows.text: He who, though dressed in fine apparel, exercises tranquillity, is quiet, subdued, restrained, chaste, and has ceased to find fault with all other beings, he indeed is a Brahmana, an ascetic (sramana), a friar (bhikshu).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Even someone dressed in expensive clothing can be a genuine ascetic if they are tranquil, self-controlled, and have stopped criticizing others.

rows.text: Is there in this world any man so restrained by humility that he does not mind reproof, as a well-trained horse the whip?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person with genuine humility and self-control doesn't get upset when corrected, similar to how a trained horse doesn't react to the whip.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of accepting reproof like a whip may encourage self-blame or shame in those already struggling with self-criticism, and could be misused to justify enduring relational harm.

rows.text: Like a well-trained horse when touched by the whip, be ye active and lively, and by faith, by virtue, by energy, by meditation, by discernment of the law you will overcome this great pain (of reproof), perfect in knowledge and in behaviour, and never forgetful.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A well-trained horse reacts to the whip with energy; similarly, you should be lively and attentive. By cultivating faith, good conduct, effort, meditation, and clear understanding, you can rise above the sting of criticism and reach full maturity in wisdom and behavior, always staying mindful.
rows.riskNotes: The whip metaphor may retraumatize survivors of abuse or relational harm, and the call to overcome pain through effort could feel dismissive of their experience.

rows.text: Well-makers lead the water (wherever they like); fletchers bend the arrow; carpenters bend a log of wood; good people fashion themselves.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as engineers channel water, fletchers straighten arrows, and carpenters shape wood, a good person works to shape their own character.

rows.text: How is there laughter, how is there joy, as this world is always burning? Why do you not seek a light, ye who are surrounded by darkness?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The world is constantly ablaze with suffering and change, so carefree laughter is out of place. Those who are in the dark should urgently seek insight.
rows.riskNotes: The stark dismissal of laughter and emphasis on a burning world may intensify hopelessness in those already feeling despair, grief, or suicidal, and could heighten anxiety for someone in panic.

rows.text: Look at this dressed-up lump, covered with wounds, joined together, sickly, full of many thoughts, which has no strength, no hold!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Consider this body: it's a decorated but fragile collection of parts, prone to illness, filled with restless thoughts, lacking lasting strength or stability.
rows.riskNotes: This stark, negative depiction of the body could deepen shame, self-blame, or despair in someone already struggling with body image, grief, or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: This body is wasted, full of sickness, and frail; this heap of corruption breaks to pieces, life indeed ends in death.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The body is worn out, prone to illness, and fragile. It eventually breaks down, and life always ends in death.
rows.riskNotes: This stark description of bodily decay and death could deepen despair or grief in someone already struggling with loss or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: Those white bones, like gourds thrown away in the autumn, what pleasure is there in looking at them?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A pile of bones, like discarded autumn gourds, holds no real pleasure for anyone who looks at them.
rows.riskNotes: The stark imagery of discarded bones may deepen hopelessness for someone in despair or grieving, and could reinforce suicidal thoughts by emphasizing the body's impermanence and lack of value.

rows.text: After a stronghold has been made of the bones, it is covered with flesh and blood, and there dwell in it old age and death, pride and deceit.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The body is described as a fortress of bones filled with flesh and blood, where aging, death, arrogance, and deception make their home.
rows.riskNotes: The vivid imagery of death dwelling within the body may deepen hopelessness for those in despair or grieving, and could reinforce suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: The brilliant chariots of kings are destroyed, the body also approaches destruction, but the virtue of good people never approaches destruction,—thus do the good say to the good.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Even the finest material things and the body itself break down, but a person's moral goodness endures, as the wise remind each other.
rows.riskNotes: The vivid mention of the body's destruction may intensify pain for someone in fresh grief.

rows.text: A man who has learnt little, grows old like an ox; his flesh grows, but his knowledge does not grow.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who hasn't gained much understanding grows old like an ox: their body gets bigger, but their mind doesn't develop.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could wound someone already feeling worthless or despairing about their life by comparing them to an ox and emphasizing their lack of growth.

rows.text: Looking for the maker of this tabernacle, I shall have to run through a course of many births, so long as I do not find (him); and painful is birth again and again. But now, maker of the tabernacle, thou hast been seen; thou shalt not make up this tabernacle again. All thy rafters are broken, thy ridge-pole is sundered; the mind, approaching the Eternal (visankhara, nirvana), has attained to the extinction of all desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: After searching through many lives for the cause of repeated birth and suffering, the seeker has finally seen it. Now that the source is known, the cycle is broken, and the mind has reached a state free from all craving.

rows.text: Men who have not observed proper discipline, and have not gained treasure in their youth, perish like old herons in a lake without fish.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Those who do not cultivate self-discipline and inner resources in their youth become destitute in old age, like herons in a lake without fish.
rows.riskNotes: The passage may deepen feelings of failure and hopelessness in someone already struggling with regret or self-blame about their past.

rows.text: Men who have not observed proper discipline, and have not gained treasure in their youth, lie, like broken bows, sighing after the past.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Those who neglected self-discipline and failed to build inner wealth in their youth spend their later years filled with regret, like useless broken bows.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of regret and self-blame in someone already struggling with shame or despair over past choices.

rows.text: If a man hold himself dear, let him watch himself carefully; during one at least out of the three watches a wise man should be watchful.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who values their own well-being should carefully monitor their thoughts and actions. A wise individual remains alert for at least one part of the day.

rows.text: Let each man direct himself first to what is proper, then let him teach others; thus a wise man will not suffer.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should first correct their own actions before trying to instruct others. Doing so prevents suffering for the wise.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may reinforce feelings of inadequacy in someone already struggling with shame or self-blame, as it implies suffering results from not first directing oneself properly.

rows.text: If a man make himself as he teaches others to be, then, being himself well subdued, he may subdue (others); one's own self is indeed difficult to subdue.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should align their own behavior with the standards they set for others. Only when one has truly mastered oneself can one effectively guide others, because self-discipline is extremely difficult.

rows.text: Self is the lord of self, who else could be the lord? With self well subdued, a man finds a lord such as few can find.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person is their own master; no one else can be. When someone has trained and controlled themselves well, they achieve a kind of mastery that is rare.

rows.text: The evil done by oneself, self-begotten, self-bred, crushes the foolish, as a diamond breaks a precious stone.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The harm a person does to themselves through their own actions eventually destroys them, just as a diamond can shatter a gemstone.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may intensify feelings of self-blame and despair in someone already struggling with shame or suicidal thoughts, as it attributes crushing suffering to one's own evil actions.

rows.text: He whose wickedness is very great brings himself down to that state where his enemy wishes him to be, as a creeper does with the tree which it surrounds.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person's own great wrongdoing causes their own downfall, just as a parasitic vine eventually destroys the tree it clings to.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen self-condemnation for someone already struggling with shame or self-blame, as it attributes suffering directly to one's own wickedness.

rows.text: Bad deeds, and deeds hurtful to ourselves, are easy to do; what is beneficial and good, that is very difficult to do.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: It is easy to act in harmful or self-destructive ways, but doing what is truly beneficial and good takes real effort.

rows.text: The foolish man who scorns the rule of the venerable (Arahat), of the elect (Ariya), of the virtuous, and follows false doctrine, he bears fruit to his own destruction, like the fruits of the Katthaka reed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who rejects the guidance of the wise and virtuous and instead follows harmful teachings brings about their own ruin, like a plant whose fruit destroys itself.
rows.riskNotes: The imagery of bearing fruit to one's own destruction could deepen self-blame and despair in someone already feeling foolish or worthless.

rows.text: By oneself the evil is done, by oneself one suffers; by oneself evil is left undone, by oneself one is purified. Purity and impurity belong to oneself, no one can purify another.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person's harmful actions come from themselves, and they experience the suffering that results. Likewise, refraining from harm and becoming pure are one's own doing. Purity and impurity are personal; no one else can make another pure.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may be heard as blaming the sufferer for their own pain, which could deepen self-blame, shame, or despair in someone already feeling responsible for their hardship.

rows.text: Let no one forget his own duty for the sake of another's, however great; let a man, after he has discerned his own duty, be always attentive to his duty.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: One should not neglect their own responsibilities for another's, no matter how important the other's may appear, but instead discern and stay focused on one's own duty.

rows.text: Do not follow the evil law! Do not live on in thoughtlessness! Do not follow false doctrine! Be not a friend of the world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Steer clear of harmful actions, mindless living, mistaken beliefs, and worldly attachments.
rows.riskNotes: The admonishing tone may be heard as condemnation by someone already struggling with self-blame or acute shame, reinforcing feelings of unworthiness.

rows.text: Rouse thyself! do not be idle! Follow the law of virtue! The virtuous rests in bliss in this world and in the next.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Stop being idle and make an effort. Follow a virtuous way of life. The virtuous person experiences happiness in this life and the next.

rows.text: Follow the law of virtue; do not follow that of sin. The virtuous rests in bliss in this world and in the next.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Live by ethical principles, not by harmful actions. A person who does good finds happiness both now and after death.

rows.text: Look upon the world as a bubble, look upon it as a mirage: the king of death does not see him who thus looks down upon the world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The world is as fleeting and insubstantial as a bubble or a mirage. Seeing it this way, one escapes the reach of death.

rows.text: Come, look at this glittering world, like unto a royal chariot; the foolish are immersed in it, but the wise do not touch it.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The world appears glittering and appealing, like a royal chariot. Those who lack wisdom become absorbed in it, while the wise remain unattached.
rows.riskNotes: The labeling of those immersed in the world as 'foolish' could intensify shame or self-blame in a person already feeling unworthy.

rows.text: He who formerly was reckless and afterwards became sober, brightens up this world, like the moon when freed from clouds.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who used to act carelessly but later becomes disciplined and aware brings light to the world, just as the moon shines when clouds clear.

rows.text: He whose evil deeds are covered by good deeds, brightens up this world, like the moon when freed from clouds.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who offsets their past wrongdoings with good actions brings light to the world, just as the moon shines when clouds clear away.

rows.text: This world is dark, few only can see here; a few only go to heaven, like birds escaped from the net.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Most people live in ignorance and cannot see the truth. Only a rare few gain insight and free themselves from the traps of desire and attachment, like birds escaping a net.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of hopelessness for someone already in despair or experiencing suicidal ideation by emphasizing the world's darkness and the rarity of liberation.

rows.text: The swans go on the path of the sun, they go through the ether by means of their miraculous power; the wise are led out of this world, when they have conquered Mara and his train.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as swans fly through the sky by their own strength, those who overcome inner struggles and temptations are freed from the world.
rows.riskNotes: The phrase 'led out of this world' may be misinterpreted by someone with suicidal ideation as endorsing escape from life, potentially reinforcing harmful thoughts.

rows.text: If a man has transgressed one law, and speaks lies, and scoffs at another world, there is no evil he will not do.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who breaks even one moral rule, tells lies, and dismisses the idea of an afterlife will be capable of any wrongdoing.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could reinforce feelings of irredeemable evil in someone already struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair, potentially deepening hopelessness.

rows.text: The uncharitable do not go to the world of the gods; fools only do not praise liberality; a wise man rejoices in liberality, and through it becomes blessed in the other world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Those who are not generous do not reach a heavenly state. Only fools fail to appreciate generosity, while the wise delight in it and through it gain happiness in the afterlife.

rows.text: Better than sovereignty over the earth, better than going to heaven, better than lordship over all worlds, is the reward of the first step in holiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The benefit of even the initial stage of spiritual awakening surpasses ruling the earth, reaching heaven, or having power over all realms.

rows.text: He whose conquest is not conquered again, into whose conquest no one in this world enters, by what track can you lead him, the Awakened, the Omniscient, the trackless?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has achieved a lasting inner victory that cannot be undone or challenged by anyone in the world is beyond being influenced or led. Such a person is awake, all-knowing, and leaves no trace for others to follow.

rows.text: He whom no desire with its snares and poisons can lead astray, by what track can you lead him, the Awakened, the Omniscient, the trackless?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who is no longer misled by the traps and poisons of desire cannot be followed or influenced. Such a person is awake, all-knowing, and leaves no trail.

rows.text: Even the gods envy those who are awakened and not forgetful, who are given to meditation, who are wise, and who delight in the repose of retirement (from the world).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who are awake, mindful, meditative, wise, and happy in solitude are envied even by the gods.

rows.text: Difficult (to obtain) is the conception of men, difficult is the life of mortals, difficult is the hearing of the True Law, difficult is the birth of the Awakened (the attainment of Buddhahood).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Being born human is rare, living is hard, encountering genuine teachings is uncommon, and the appearance of an awakened person is extremely rare.
rows.riskNotes: The statement that life is difficult may deepen feelings of hopelessness for someone in despair or with suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: Not to commit any sin, to do good, and to purify one's mind, that is the teaching of (all) the Awakened.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The essential teaching of all awakened beings is to avoid harmful actions, to do what is beneficial, and to cleanse one's own mind.

rows.text: The Awakened call patience the highest penance, long-suffering the highest Nirvana; for he is not an anchorite (pravragita) who strikes others, he is not an ascetic (sramana) who insults others.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The Buddha teaches that patience and forbearance are the greatest spiritual practices. A person who harms or insults others cannot be considered a true renunciant or ascetic.
rows.riskNotes: May pressure those experiencing harm to practice patience and long-suffering, potentially invalidating their anger or need for self-protection.

rows.text: Not to blame, not to strike, to live restrained under the law, to be moderate in eating, to sleep and sit alone, and to dwell on the highest thoughts,—this is the teaching of the Awakened.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: This teaching advises avoiding blame and violence, living with discipline, eating moderately, practicing solitude, and focusing the mind on elevated thoughts.
rows.riskNotes: The instruction 'not to blame' may feel invalidating to someone processing anger at an aggressor or recovering from relational harm.

rows.text: There is no satisfying lusts, even by a shower of gold pieces; he who knows that lusts have a short taste and cause pain, he is wise;
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Even unlimited wealth cannot satisfy desires; the wise understand that desires bring only fleeting pleasure and lead to suffering.

rows.text: Even in heavenly pleasures he finds no satisfaction, the disciple who is fully awakened delights only in the destruction of all desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Even the most exquisite pleasures leave a fully awakened person unsatisfied; their joy comes solely from the complete ending of all desires.

rows.text: Men, driven by fear, go to many a refuge, to mountains and forests, to groves and sacred trees.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When people are afraid, they often look for safety in external things like mountains, forests, or sacred sites.

rows.text: But that is not a safe refuge, that is not the best refuge; a man is not delivered from all pains after having gone to that refuge.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Some refuges people seek are not truly safe or ultimate; they do not free a person from all suffering.

rows.text: He who takes refuge with Buddha, the Law, and the Church; he who, with clear understanding, sees the four holy truths:—
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person finds safety in the Buddha, his teachings, and the community, and clearly understands the four noble truths.

rows.text: Viz. pain, the origin of pain, the destruction of pain, and the eightfold holy way that leads to the quieting of pain;—
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Suffering exists, it has a cause, it can end, and there is a practical path that leads to its end.

rows.text: That is the safe refuge, that is the best refuge; having gone to that refuge, a man is delivered from all pain.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: There is a refuge that is safe and unsurpassed; when a person goes to it, they are released from all suffering.

rows.text: A supernatural person (a Buddha) is not easily found, he is not born everywhere. Wherever such a sage is born, that race prospers.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A fully awakened person is extremely rare and not born just anywhere. When such a sage does appear, the family or community they are born into flourishes.

rows.text: Happy is the arising of the awakened, happy is the teaching of the True Law, happy is peace in the church, happy is the devotion of those who are at peace.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The appearance of an awakened person, the teaching of truth, harmony in a community, and the dedication of peaceful people are all sources of genuine happiness.

rows.text: He who pays homage to those who deserve homage, whether the awakened (Buddha) or their disciples, those who have overcome the host (of evils), and crossed the flood of sorrow, he who pays homage to such as have found deliverance and know no fear, his merit can never be measured by anybody.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Honoring those who have become free from suffering and fear—whether they are fully awakened or their followers—brings a benefit so vast that no one can calculate it.

rows.text: Let us live happily then, not hating those who hate us! among men who hate us let us dwell free from hatred!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: One can live happily and without hatred, even when surrounded by people who hate.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel invalidating or pressuring to someone who is currently experiencing hatred toward an aggressor or abuser, especially if they are disclosing harm.

rows.text: Let us live happily then, free from ailments among the ailing! among men who are ailing let us dwell free from ailments!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: One can live happily and free from mental distress even while surrounded by those who are distressed.

rows.text: Let us live happily then, free from greed among the greedy! among men who are greedy let us dwell free from greed!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person can be genuinely happy without being driven by the same intense desires that consume others, even while living among them.

rows.text: Let us live happily then, though we call nothing our own! We shall be like the bright gods, feeding on happiness!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: It is possible to live joyfully while owning nothing, like the gods who are nourished by happiness itself.
rows.riskNotes: The call to live happily while owning nothing may feel invalidating to someone in fresh grief, as it could be heard as dismissing the pain of losing a loved one.

rows.text: Victory breeds hatred, for the conquered is unhappy. He who has given up both victory and defeat, he, the contented, is happy.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Winning creates resentment because the loser suffers. A person who lets go of both winning and losing finds happiness through contentment.
rows.riskNotes: The teaching that victory breeds hatred and contentment comes from giving up both victory and defeat may feel dismissive to someone in the midst of relational harm or righteous anger, as it could be perceived as minimizing their struggle.

rows.text: There is no fire like passion; there is no losing throw like hatred; there is no pain like this body; there is no happiness higher than rest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Intense desire consumes like a fire, hatred guarantees defeat, the body brings suffering, and only inner stillness offers true happiness.

rows.text: Hunger is the worst of diseases, the body the greatest of pains; if one knows this truly, that is Nirvana, the highest happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Hunger is the most severe affliction, and the physical body is the greatest source of suffering. Truly understanding this leads to the ultimate peace and happiness.

rows.text: Health is the greatest of gifts, contentedness the best riches; trust is the best of relationships, Nirvana the highest happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Good health is the most valuable gift, a contented mind is the richest treasure, trust is the strongest bond, and the liberation of Nirvana is the highest happiness.

rows.text: He who has tasted the sweetness of solitude and tranquillity, is free from fear and free from sin, while he tastes the sweetness of drinking in the law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has experienced the peace of being alone and calm is free from fear and wrongdoing, and finds joy in understanding the teachings.

rows.text: The sight of the elect (Arya) is good, to live with them is always happiness; if a man does not see fools, he will be truly happy.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Spending time with wise people brings happiness, while avoiding foolish people leads to genuine well-being.

rows.text: He who walks in the company of fools suffers a long way; company with fools, as with an enemy, is always painful; company with the wise is pleasure, like meeting with kinsfolk.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Spending time with foolish people brings lasting distress, like being with an enemy. Being with wise people is enjoyable, like being with family.

rows.text: Therefore, one ought to follow the wise, the intelligent, the learned, the much enduring, the dutiful, the elect; one ought to follow a good and wise man, as the moon follows the path of the stars.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should seek the company of those who are wise, intelligent, learned, patient, and dutiful, following a good and wise individual as the moon follows the stars.

rows.text: He who gives himself to vanity, and does not give himself to meditation, forgetting the real aim (of life) and grasping at pleasure, will in time envy him who has exerted himself in meditation.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who chases empty pleasures and neglects inner reflection loses sight of what truly matters, and will eventually feel envious of those who dedicated themselves to meditation.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of inadequacy for someone already struggling with self-blame or shame, as it implies their current lack of meditation will lead to future regret.

rows.text: Let no man ever look for what is pleasant, or what is unpleasant. Not to see what is pleasant is pain, and it is pain to see what is unpleasant.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Chasing after what feels good and trying to escape what feels bad both cause distress. When you can't get what you enjoy, it hurts; when you have to deal with what you dislike, it also hurts.

rows.text: Let, therefore, no man love anything; loss of the beloved is evil. Those who love nothing and hate nothing, have no fetters.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Don't get attached to anything, because losing what you love brings suffering. If you neither cling to things nor push them away, you won't be trapped.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may harm those grieving a loss or struggling with self-blame by implying that love itself causes suffering, and could invalidate the anger of someone processing harm.

rows.text: From pleasure comes grief, from pleasure comes fear; he who is free from pleasure knows neither grief nor fear.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Pleasure leads to sorrow and fear; when someone is no longer attached to pleasure, they are free from both.
rows.riskNotes: Someone grieving a loss may feel blamed for their pain, as the verse suggests grief arises from one's own pleasure.

rows.text: From affection comes grief, from affection comes fear; he who is free from affection knows neither grief nor fear.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Emotional attachment to people or things brings sorrow and anxiety. Letting go of that attachment removes those painful feelings.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may wound those grieving a loss by implying their affection is the cause of their pain, and could be misinterpreted by someone in suicidal crisis as advocating detachment from life.

rows.text: From lust comes grief, from lust comes fear; he who is free from lust knows neither grief nor fear.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Desire brings sorrow and fear. When desire is absent, sorrow and fear do not arise.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may be harmful to those experiencing shame, self-blame, or despair, as it attributes grief and fear to personal lust, potentially reinforcing feelings of fault and hopelessness.

rows.text: From love comes grief, from love comes fear; he who is free from love knows neither grief nor fear.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Clinging to people or things leads to sorrow and fear. When a person is free from such clinging, they do not experience those painful emotions.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may wound someone grieving a loss by implying their grief is a direct result of love, and could intensify self-blame, shame, or hopelessness in those already feeling at fault for their pain or considering self-harm.

rows.text: From greed comes grief, from greed comes fear; he who is free from greed knows neither grief nor fear.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Craving leads to distress and anxiety. Without craving, these troubles do not arise.
rows.riskNotes: A person in self-blame or fresh grief might feel that their suffering is being attributed to their own greed, deepening shame or invalidating their pain.

rows.text: He who possesses virtue and intelligence, who is just, speaks the truth, and does what is his own business, him the world will hold dear.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is virtuous, intelligent, fair, truthful, and attends to their own responsibilities is valued by others.

rows.text: He in whom a desire for the Ineffable (Nirvana) has sprung up, who is satisfied in his mind, and whose thoughts are not bewildered by love, he is called urdhvamsrotas (carried upwards by the stream).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has developed a genuine longing for the unconditioned, who is content within, and whose thinking is no longer tangled by attachment, is said to be moving upstream toward liberation.

rows.text: Kinsmen, friends, and lovers salute a man who has been long away, and returns safe from afar.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When someone returns home safely after being away for a long time, their family and friends greet them with affection.

rows.text: In like manner his good works receive him who has done good, and has gone from this world to the other;—as kinsmen receive a friend on his return.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person who has lived a good life passes away, their good deeds welcome them into the next existence, much like family members greet a returning friend.
rows.riskNotes: The imagery of death and a welcoming afterlife may intensify grief for those in fresh loss, and could be dangerously misinterpreted by someone with suicidal ideation.

rows.text: Let a man leave anger, let him forsake pride, let him overcome all bondage! No sufferings befall the man who is not attached to name and form, and who calls nothing his own.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Abandoning anger, pride, and all forms of clinging leads to freedom from suffering. The one who is unattached to identity and possessions experiences no distress.
rows.riskNotes: The implication that suffering arises from attachment may deepen self-blame and shame in those already feeling at fault, and the call to abandon anger could feel dismissive to victims of harm or those in despair.

rows.text: He who holds back rising anger like a rolling chariot, him I call a real driver; other people are but holding the reins.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who can suppress rising anger, like stopping a moving chariot, is truly in control; others only appear to hold the reins.
rows.riskNotes: For someone experiencing rage at an aggressor or harm from a relationship, this verse may feel like it dismisses their anger and pressures them to suppress it.

rows.text: Let a man overcome anger by love, let him overcome evil by good; let him overcome the greedy by liberality, the liar by truth!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Respond to hostility with kindness, to wrongdoing with goodness, to greed with generosity, and to dishonesty with truthfulness.
rows.riskNotes: This call to respond to harm with love may feel dismissive to those processing justified anger or relational trauma, especially if they have recently disclosed abuse.

rows.text: Speak the truth, do not yield to anger; give, if thou art asked for little; by these three steps thou wilt go near the gods.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Speaking truthfully, not succumbing to anger, and giving when asked for little are three actions that bring one close to the gods.
rows.riskNotes: The instruction to not yield to anger and to give when asked may feel invalidating to someone processing justified rage or recovering from relational harm.

rows.text: The sages who injure nobody, and who always control their body, they will go to the unchangeable place (Nirvana), where, if they have gone, they will suffer no more.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Wise people who harm no one and keep their actions in check reach a state of lasting peace where suffering ends.

rows.text: Those who are ever watchful, who study day and night, and who strive after Nirvana, their passions will come to an end.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who remain attentive, dedicate themselves to learning day and night, and pursue complete freedom from suffering will eventually overcome their inner cravings.

rows.text: This is an old saying, O Atula, this is not only of to-day: `They blame him who sits silent, they blame him who speaks much, they also blame him who says little; there is no one on earth who is not blamed.'
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People will criticize you whether you stay quiet, talk a lot, or speak moderately. No one escapes blame entirely.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may reinforce feelings of being inherently blameworthy for someone in acute shame or self-blame, and deepen hopelessness for someone in despair.

rows.text: There never was, there never will be, nor is there now, a man who is always blamed, or a man who is always praised.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: No one is ever universally blamed or universally praised; such a person has never existed and never will.

rows.text: But he whom those who discriminate praise continually day after day, as without blemish, wise, rich in knowledge and virtue, who would dare to blame him, like a coin made of gold from the Gambu river? Even the gods praise him, he is praised even by Brahman.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is faultless, wise, and virtuous is praised by those with good judgment and even by the gods. Such a person is beyond reproach, like a piece of pure gold.

rows.text: Beware of bodily anger, and control thy body! Leave the sins of the body, and with thy body practise virtue!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Guard against anger that shows in your physical actions. Control your body, abandon harmful physical behavior, and instead do good through your actions.
rows.riskNotes: The mention of 'sins of the body' may intensify feelings of shame or self-blame in someone already struggling with those states.

rows.text: Beware of the anger of the tongue, and control thy tongue! Leave the sins of the tongue, and practise virtue with thy tongue!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Guard your speech when you're angry. Stop harmful talk and instead use your words to do good.
rows.riskNotes: For someone experiencing rage at an aggressor or harm from a relationship, this command to control the tongue may feel like an invalidation of their justified anger or a pressure to remain silent.

rows.text: Beware of the anger of the mind, and control thy mind! Leave the sins of the mind, and practise virtue with thy mind!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Watch out for mental anger and take charge of your mind. Abandon unwholesome mental patterns and cultivate wholesome ones.
rows.riskNotes: The phrase 'sins of the mind' may deepen shame or self-blame, and the call to control anger could feel dismissive to those processing justified rage or relational harm.

rows.text: The wise who control their body, who control their tongue, the wise who control their mind, are indeed well controlled.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Those who are wise exercise restraint over their physical actions, their words, and their thoughts; such people have truly mastered themselves.

rows.text: Thou art now like a sear leaf, the messengers of death (Yama) have come near to thee; thou standest at the door of thy departure, and thou hast no provision for thy journey.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person is like a dried-up leaf; death's messengers are near, and they stand at the point of departure without having made any preparations for the journey ahead.
rows.riskNotes: This stark warning of death and unpreparedness could intensify despair, panic, or suicidal thoughts, and may wound those grieving or feeling self-blame.

rows.text: Make thyself an island, work hard, be wise! When thy impurities are blown away, and thou art free from guilt, thou wilt enter into the heavenly world of the elect (Ariya).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Rely on yourself, put in diligent effort, and develop wisdom. When you have removed your inner defilements and are free from wrongdoing, you will attain the noble state.
rows.riskNotes: The mention of being 'free from guilt' may intensify feelings of shame or self-blame in someone already struggling with those states.

rows.text: Thy life has come to an end, thou art come near to death (Yama), there is no resting-place for thee on the road, and thou hast no provision for thy journey.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Your time is almost up, death is approaching, and you have neither a place to pause nor any resources for the journey beyond.
rows.riskNotes: This stark warning of imminent death and unpreparedness could deepen despair, panic, or self-blame in someone already struggling with mortality, grief, or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: Make thyself an island, work hard, be wise! When thy impurities are blown away, and thou art free from guilt, thou wilt not enter again into birth and decay.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Become your own refuge, apply yourself diligently, and develop understanding. Once your inner stains are removed and you are free of moral fault, you escape the cycle of birth and decay.
rows.riskNotes: The mention of being 'free from guilt' and 'impurities' may intensify feelings of shame or self-blame in someone already struggling with those states.

rows.text: Let a wise man blow off the impurities of his self, as a smith blows off the impurities of silver one by one, little by little, and from time to time.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person with insight gradually removes their own flaws, just as a metalworker refines silver by repeatedly removing impurities.

rows.text: As the impurity which springs from the iron, when it springs from it, destroys it; thus do a transgressor's own works lead him to the evil path.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as rust that forms on iron eventually eats away and destroys the iron itself, a person's own harmful actions lead them down a path of suffering.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen self-blame and hopelessness in someone already feeling like a transgressor, potentially worsening shame, despair, or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: The taint of prayers is non-repetition; the taint of houses, non-repair; the taint of the body is sloth; the taint of a watchman, thoughtlessness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Neglecting to repeat prayers, failing to maintain a house, laziness in the body, and inattentiveness in a guard are all considered forms of impurity or flaw.
rows.riskNotes: A person already struggling with self-blame or shame may interpret the mention of sloth as a taint as a personal condemnation, deepening feelings of inadequacy.

rows.text: Bad conduct is the taint of woman, greediness the taint of a benefactor; tainted are all evil ways in this world and in the next.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Wrong behavior stains a person, just as greed stains a giver; all harmful actions are impure both in this life and beyond.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's singling out of women as having a taint of bad conduct may reinforce shame and self-blame in women, especially those who have experienced relational harm or abuse.

rows.text: But there is a taint worse than all taints,—ignorance is the greatest taint. O mendicants! throw off that taint, and become taintless!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Ignorance is the most harmful defilement. Cast it off and become free from all defilements.
rows.riskNotes: The labeling of ignorance as a 'taint' may intensify shame and self-blame in those already feeling flawed, and the exhortation to 'throw it off' could deepen despair in someone who feels incapable of change.

rows.text: Life is easy to live for a man who is without shame, a crow hero, a mischief-maker, an insulting, bold, and wretched fellow.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who lacks shame, acts audaciously, causes trouble, and is insulting and contemptible finds life easy.
rows.riskNotes: This passage's harsh condemnation of shamelessness may deepen shame and self-blame in readers already struggling with feelings of unworthiness or moral failure.

rows.text: But life is hard to live for a modest man, who always looks for what is pure, who is disinterested, quiet, spotless, and intelligent.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: It is difficult to live as a person who is humble, seeks what is pure, remains detached, calm, faultless, and discerning.

rows.text: He who destroys life, who speaks untruth, who in this world takes what is not given him, who goes to another man's wife;
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who takes life, tells lies, takes what isn't given, and has sex with another's spouse.
rows.riskNotes: Listing these transgressions may intensify shame or self-blame in someone who has committed similar acts.

rows.text: And the man who gives himself to drinking intoxicating liquors, he, even in this world, digs up his own root.
