rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who habitually drinks alcohol destroys their own well-being and future.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen shame and self-blame in someone struggling with alcohol, and could reinforce despair by framing their behavior as self-destruction.

rows.text: O man, know this, that the unrestrained are in a bad state; take care that greediness and vice do not bring thee to grief for a long time!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who lack self-control are in a bad situation. Greed and wrongdoing can cause long-lasting suffering, so be careful.
rows.riskNotes: This warning that unrestrained greed and vice lead to long grief could intensify self-blame and shame in someone already feeling at fault for their suffering.

rows.text: The world gives according to their faith or according to their pleasure: if a man frets about the food and the drink given to others, he will find no rest either by day or by night.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People give according to their own faith or preference. If someone is envious of what others receive, they will be restless day and night.

rows.text: He in whom that feeling is destroyed, and taken out with the very root, finds rest by day and by night.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When someone completely uproots and destroys a harmful desire, they experience uninterrupted rest.

rows.text: There is no fire like passion, there is no shark like hatred, there is no snare like folly, there is no torrent like greed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Intense desire, anger, ignorance, and greed are the most destructive forces one can encounter.

rows.text: The fault of others is easily perceived, but that of oneself is difficult to perceive; a man winnows his neighbour's faults like chaff, but his own fault he hides, as a cheat hides the bad die from the gambler.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: It's much easier to spot other people's mistakes than to see your own. People tend to expose the faults of others while hiding their own, like a dishonest gambler concealing a losing die.
rows.riskNotes: The simile of a cheat hiding a bad die may feel accusatory and deepen shame or self-blame in someone already struggling with guilt over their own faults.

rows.text: If a man looks after the faults of others, and is always inclined to be offended, his own passions will grow, and he is far from the destruction of passions.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who habitually notices others' faults and is easily offended will see their own negative emotions grow, and they will remain far from freeing themselves from those emotions.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may invalidate the feelings of someone who has been harmed, implying that their offense is a personal failing that fuels suffering.

rows.text: There is no path through the air, a man is not a Samana by outward acts. The world delights in vanity, the Tathagatas (the Buddhas) are free from vanity.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Spiritual progress isn't achieved through shortcuts or outward displays. True awakening is about inner freedom from delusion, not external appearances.
rows.riskNotes: The passage is a calm observation about the nature of spiritual attainment and vanity, unlikely to wound a fragile person.

rows.text: There is no path through the air, a man is not a Samana by outward acts. No creatures are eternal; but the awakened (Buddha) are never shaken.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: There is no easy escape from suffering, and external actions alone don't define a true seeker. All beings are impermanent, but the awakened ones are unshakable.
rows.riskNotes: The blunt statement that no creatures are eternal may feel dismissive or painful to someone in fresh grief.

rows.text: A man is not just if he carries a matter by violence; no, he who distinguishes both right and wrong, who is learned and leads others, not by violence, but by law and equity, and who is guarded by the law and intelligent, he is called just.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: True justice isn't about forcing an outcome through aggression. A just person understands right and wrong, is knowledgeable, and guides others by fairness and law, not by violence. Such a person is both law-abiding and discerning.

rows.text: A man is not learned because he talks much; he who is patient, free from hatred and fear, he is called learned.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Being talkative doesn't make someone wise. Real wisdom is shown by a person who is patient and free from hostility and fear.

rows.text: A man is not a supporter of the law because he talks much; even if a man has learnt little, but sees the law bodily, he is a supporter of the law, a man who never neglects the law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person is not a genuine follower of the teaching simply by speaking a lot about it; even someone with little learning who directly perceives and lives the teaching is a true follower who never abandons it.

rows.text: A man is not an elder because his head is grey; his age may be ripe, but he is called `Old-in-vain.'
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Grey hair or old age alone does not make someone a true elder. Without wisdom, a long life is empty and unworthy of respect.

rows.text: He in whom there is truth, virtue, love, restraint, moderation, he who is free from impurity and is wise, he is called an elder.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: True elderhood is not about age but about having integrity, ethical conduct, goodwill, self-control, balance, inner purity, and insight.

rows.text: An envious greedy, dishonest man does not become respectable by means of much talking only, or by the beauty of his complexion.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is envious, greedy, and dishonest does not become respected just by talking a lot or having a pleasing appearance.

rows.text: He in whom all this is destroyed, and taken out with the very root, he, when freed from hatred and wise, is called respectable.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who has fully uprooted hatred and is wise is considered honorable.

rows.text: Not by tonsure does an undisciplined man who speaks falsehood become a Samana; can a man be a Samana who is still held captive by desire and greediness?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Shaving one's head does not make someone a genuine ascetic if they remain undisciplined, speak falsely, and are still enslaved by desire and greed.
rows.riskNotes: A person struggling with shame or self-blame may interpret this as confirmation of their unworthiness, deepening feelings of being a fraud.

rows.text: He who always quiets the evil, whether small or large, he is called a Samana (a quiet man), because he has quieted all evil.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who consistently calms and eliminates harmful tendencies, no matter how minor or major, is truly a peaceful person because they have overcome all inner negativity.

rows.text: A man is not a mendicant (Bhikshu) simply because he asks others for alms; he who adopts the whole law is a Bhikshu, not he who only begs.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Being a monk isn't about begging for alms; it's about embracing the entire teaching. The label means nothing if the inner practice isn't there.

rows.text: He who is above good and evil, who is chaste, who with knowledge passes through the world, he indeed is called a Bhikshu.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true monk is one who has moved beyond the labels of good and evil, lives with self-control, and goes through life with clear understanding.

rows.text: A man is not a Muni because he observes silence (mona, i.e. mauna), if he is foolish and ignorant; but the wise who, taking the balance, chooses the good and avoids evil, he is a Muni, and is a Muni thereby; he who in this world weighs both sides is called a Muni.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person is not a sage simply because they keep silent, if they lack understanding. A true sage is one who carefully examines both sides and chooses what is good while avoiding evil.

rows.text: A man is not an elect (Ariya) because he injures living creatures; because he has pity on all living creatures, therefore is a man called Ariya.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person is not noble because they harm living beings; one is noble because they show compassion to all living beings.

rows.text: Not only by discipline and vows, not only by much learning, not by entering into a trance, not by sleeping alone, do I earn the happiness of release which no worldling can know. Bhikshu, be not confident as long as thou hast not attained the extinction of desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: External practices like discipline, learning, meditation, and solitude are not enough to achieve liberation. True freedom only comes when all craving is completely extinguished, so one should not become complacent before that point.
rows.riskNotes: The warning against complacency may deepen feelings of inadequacy and hopelessness in those already struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair.

rows.text: The best of ways is the eightfold; the best of truths the four words; the best of virtues passionlessness; the best of men he who has eyes to see.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The Eightfold Path is the best way to live; the Four Noble Truths are the most important teachings; freedom from craving is the highest virtue; and the person with insight is the best kind of person.

rows.text: This is the way, there is no other that leads to the purifying of intelligence. Go on this way! Everything else is the deceit of Mara (the tempter).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: There is only one way to cleanse and clarify your understanding. Walk that path, because every other direction is a trick of the tempter.

rows.text: If you go on this way, you will make an end of pain! The way was preached by me, when I had understood the removal of the thorns (in the flesh).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Following this path leads to the end of suffering. The Buddha taught it after he understood how to remove the sources of pain.

rows.text: You yourself must make an effort. The Tathagatas (Buddhas) are only preachers. The thoughtful who enter the way are freed from the bondage of Mara.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Liberation depends on your own effort; teachers can only point the way, and those who are reflective and follow it free themselves from inner bondage.

rows.text: `All created things perish,' he who knows and sees this becomes passive in pain; this is the way to purity.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Everything that arises eventually ceases. Seeing this truth clearly leads to a calm acceptance of suffering, which is the path to inner purity.
rows.riskNotes: The instruction to become passive in pain can feel like a dismissal of legitimate suffering, potentially deepening shame, self-blame, or hopelessness, and may be dangerous for someone in acute grief or suicidal crisis.

rows.text: `All created things are grief and pain,' he who knows and sees this becomes passive in pain; this is the way that leads to purity.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: All conditioned things are inherently unsatisfactory and painful. Seeing this clearly, a person stops resisting pain and becomes inwardly still, which is the path to purification.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen despair or hopelessness in someone already feeling that life is only pain, and could be particularly harmful for those grieving or considering suicide.

rows.text: `All forms are unreal,' he who knows and sees this becomes passive in pain; this is the way that leads to purity.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Everything we perceive is insubstantial and fleeting. Recognizing this truth allows a person to face suffering without resistance, which is the way to cleanse the mind.
rows.riskNotes: The suggestion to become passive in pain may be misinterpreted as endorsing helplessness, which could deepen despair or suicidal ideation, and may invalidate the acute pain of fresh grief.

rows.text: He who does not rouse himself when it is time to rise, who, though young and strong, is full of sloth, whose will and thought are weak, that lazy and idle man will never find the way to knowledge.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who fails to get going when the time is right, who is sluggish despite youth and strength, and whose resolve and thinking are feeble—that idle person will never find the way to true understanding.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of worthlessness and hopelessness in someone already struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair about their perceived laziness or lack of progress.

rows.text: Watching his speech, well restrained in mind, let a man never commit any wrong with his body! Let a man but keep these three roads of action clear, and he will achieve the way which is taught by the wise.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should be careful with their words, keep their thoughts under control, and never do anything harmful with their body. By keeping these three areas of action clear, they will follow the path that wise people teach.

rows.text: Through zeal knowledge is gotten, through lack of zeal knowledge is lost; let a man who knows this double path of gain and loss thus place himself that knowledge may grow.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Knowledge comes from consistent effort, and without effort it fades. Understanding this, a person should arrange their life so that their understanding continues to deepen.

rows.text: Cut down the whole forest (of lust), not a tree only! Danger comes out of the forest (of lust). When you have cut down both the forest (of lust) and its undergrowth, then, Bhikshus, you will be rid of the forest and free!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Eliminate craving completely, not just its obvious forms. Danger comes from craving itself. Only by uprooting all desires, even subtle ones, can you be truly free.
rows.riskNotes: The exhortation to completely eradicate lust may intensify shame and self-blame in someone already struggling with feelings of moral failure or impurity.

rows.text: So long as the love of man towards women, even the smallest, is not destroyed, so long is his mind in bondage, as the calf that drinks milk is to its mother.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: As long as a person has even a trace of romantic or sexual desire, their mind remains trapped, just like a calf stays tied to its mother because of milk.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen shame or self-blame in someone struggling with natural desires, and could reinforce a sense of hopeless bondage for those in despair.

rows.text: Cut out the love of self, like an autumn lotus, with thy hand! Cherish the road of peace. Nirvana has been shown by Sugata (Buddha).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Remove self-centered attachment as easily as plucking an autumn lotus. Value the path to peace; the Buddha has shown the way to liberation.
rows.riskNotes: The command to cut out love of self may be misinterpreted as self-rejection, potentially deepening shame, self-blame, or despair in someone already struggling with self-worth or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: `Here I shall dwell in the rain, here in winter and summer,' thus the fool meditates, and does not think of his death.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who lacks wisdom makes detailed plans for where to live in each season but never considers that death could intervene at any moment.

rows.text: Death comes and carries off that man, praised for his children and flocks, his mind distracted, as a flood carries off a sleeping village.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person absorbed in family and possessions, with a scattered mind, is suddenly overtaken by death, like a flood sweeping away a sleeping village.
rows.riskNotes: The sudden, violent imagery of death sweeping a person away may deepen distress for those already feeling overwhelmed by loss, hopelessness, or fear of death.

rows.text: Sons are no help, nor a father, nor relations; there is no help from kinsfolk for one whom death has seized.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When death comes, family members like children, parents, and relatives are unable to offer any protection or assistance.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of isolation and hopelessness for someone grieving a loss or experiencing despair or suicidal thoughts, as it emphasizes the ultimate helplessness of loved ones in the face of death.

rows.text: A wise and good man who knows the meaning of this, should quickly clear the way that leads to Nirvana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who truly understands the teaching should act without delay to remove whatever hinders their progress toward complete freedom.

rows.text: If by leaving a small pleasure one sees a great pleasure, let a wise man leave the small pleasure, and look to the great.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: If giving up a minor enjoyment leads to a much greater benefit, a sensible person will let go of the minor one and aim for the greater.

rows.text: He who, by causing pain to others, wishes to obtain pleasure for himself, he, entangled in the bonds of hatred, will never be free from hatred.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who hurts others to gain happiness for themselves gets caught in hatred and cannot escape it.

rows.text: What ought to be done is neglected, what ought not to be done is done; the desires of unruly, thoughtless people are always increasing.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who are careless and lack discipline ignore their responsibilities and do what they shouldn't, which only makes their cravings grow stronger.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of guilt and inadequacy in someone already struggling with self-blame or acute shame, as it highlights neglected duties and wrong actions.

rows.text: But they whose whole watchfulness is always directed to their body, who do not follow what ought not to be done, and who steadfastly do what ought to be done, the desires of such watchful and wise people will come to an end.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who constantly pay attention to their physical actions, avoid harmful behaviors, and consistently do what is beneficial will see their cravings fade away.

rows.text: A true Brahmana goes scatheless, though he have killed father and mother, and two valiant kings, though he has destroyed a kingdom with all its subjects.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: An enlightened person remains untouched by harm even after uprooting the deepest inner defilements—like craving and fixed views—along with all the attachments that come with them.
rows.riskNotes: The graphic mention of killing parents and destroying a kingdom could deeply wound someone in grief, shame, or despair, and may be dangerous for those with suicidal thoughts or a history of violence.

rows.text: A true Brahmana goes scatheless, though he have killed father and mother, and two holy kings, and an eminent man besides.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A truly liberated person remains unharmed even after destroying the deepest inner forces—like craving, conceit, and mistaken beliefs—that keep one trapped in suffering.
rows.riskNotes: The literal mention of killing parents may be deeply distressing to those grieving a parent, experiencing suicidal thoughts, or processing abuse.

rows.text: The disciples of Gotama (Buddha) are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on Buddha.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Those who follow the Buddha stay continuously aware, with their attention always directed toward the Buddha.

rows.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on the law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The followers of the Buddha stay alert and keep their minds continually on the teachings.

rows.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on the church.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The followers of the Buddha stay alert and keep their minds constantly on the community of practitioners.

rows.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on their body.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The followers of the Buddha stay alert and keep their attention continuously on the physical body.

rows.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their mind day and night always delights in compassion.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Those who follow the Buddha remain attentive at all times, and their thoughts are always pleased by compassion.

rows.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their mind day and night always delights in meditation.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Those who follow the Buddha remain attentive and take constant pleasure in meditative practice.

rows.text: It is hard to leave the world (to become a friar), it is hard to enjoy the world; hard is the monastery, painful are the houses; painful it is to dwell with equals (to share everything in common) and the itinerant mendicant is beset with pain. Therefore let no man be an itinerant mendicant and he will not be beset with pain.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The verse points out that every lifestyle—whether a householder or a wandering monk—brings its own forms of hardship and pain. It warns that simply changing your external situation does not free you from suffering, so one should not undertake the mendicant life expecting an easy escape.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's emphasis on pervasive pain and its suggestion to avoid the mendicant life to escape suffering could deepen hopelessness in someone already experiencing despair or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: Whatever place a faithful, virtuous, celebrated, and wealthy man chooses, there he is respected.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is trustworthy, morally upright, well-regarded, and prosperous is honored wherever they go.

rows.text: Good people shine from afar, like the snowy mountains; bad people are not seen, like arrows shot by night.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Virtuous individuals stand out clearly, while those who are immoral remain hidden and unrecognized.
rows.riskNotes: The contrast may reinforce feelings of worthlessness or invisibility in someone already struggling with shame or self-blame.

rows.text: He alone who, without ceasing, practises the duty of sitting alone and sleeping alone, he, subduing himself, will rejoice in the destruction of all desires alone, as if living in a forest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who consistently trains in solitude and self-control, overcoming all desires, experiences joy alone as if living in a forest.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's celebration of solitary practice could reinforce isolation and be misinterpreted as endorsing withdrawal for someone experiencing suicidal ideation.

rows.text: He who says what is not, goes to hell; he also who, having done a thing, says I have not done it. After death both are equal, they are men with evil deeds in the next world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who tells lies and a person who denies their own actions both face the same fate: they are considered evildoers and suffer in the afterlife.
rows.riskNotes: The stark warning of hell for lying and denial could intensify self-condemnation and hopelessness, and may be dangerous for someone already contemplating suicide.

rows.text: Many men whose shoulders are covered with the yellow gown are ill-conditioned and unrestrained; such evil-doers by their evil deeds go to hell.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Many who wear the yellow robe are undisciplined and wicked; their evil actions lead them to a state of suffering.
rows.riskNotes: This passage's condemnation of evil-doers to hell may intensify self-blame and hopelessness in someone already feeling shame, despair, or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: Better it would be to swallow a heated iron ball, like flaring fire, than that a bad unrestrained fellow should live on the charity of the land.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Swallowing a red-hot iron ball would be preferable to living off the charity of others while being corrupt and lacking self-control.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's vivid condemnation of a 'bad unrestrained fellow' and its suggestion that a painful death is preferable to being a burden could deeply wound someone already struggling with shame, self-blame, despair, or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: Four things does a wreckless man gain who covets his neighbour's wife,—a bad reputation, an uncomfortable bed, thirdly, punishment, and lastly, hell.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who recklessly desires another's spouse will experience disgrace, discomfort, punishment, and a hellish state.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen shame and self-blame in someone already struggling with guilt over sexual misconduct or lustful thoughts.

rows.text: There is bad reputation, and the evil way (to hell), there is the short pleasure of the frightened in the arms of the frightened, and the king imposes heavy punishment; therefore let no man think of his neighbour's wife.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Adultery leads to a bad reputation, a path to suffering, fleeting pleasure mixed with fear, and severe punishment from authorities; therefore, do not entertain lustful thoughts about another person's spouse.
rows.riskNotes: Its vivid warning of hell and punishment may deepen shame, self-blame, or despair in someone already burdened by guilt over past infidelity or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: As a grass-blade, if badly grasped, cuts the arm, badly-practised asceticism leads to hell.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A blade of grass can cut the hand if grasped carelessly; similarly, asceticism practiced wrongly leads to harmful results.
rows.riskNotes: The threat of hell for imperfect practice can deepen self-blame, shame, and hopelessness, and may be acutely dangerous for someone already in suicidal crisis.

rows.text: An act carelessly performed, a broken vow, and hesitating obedience to discipline, all this brings no great reward.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Careless actions, broken promises, and half-hearted discipline don't produce meaningful results.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of inadequacy for someone already struggling with self-blame or acute shame, as it attributes lack of reward to personal failings.

rows.text: If anything is to be done, let a man do it, let him attack it vigorously! A careless pilgrim only scatters the dust of his passions more widely.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a task needs doing, tackle it with full energy. A careless person only stirs up their own inner turmoil.
rows.riskNotes: The call to vigorous action may overwhelm someone in despair or panic, and the implication that carelessness causes suffering could deepen self-blame or shame.

rows.text: An evil deed is better left undone, for a man repents of it afterwards; a good deed is better done, for having done it, one does not repent.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Harmful actions lead to regret, so they are best avoided. Beneficial actions do not cause regret, so they are worth doing.
rows.riskNotes: May intensify guilt for someone already consumed by shame or self-blame over past actions.

rows.text: Like a well-guarded frontier fort, with defences within and without, so let a man guard himself. Not a moment should escape, for they who allow the right moment to pass, suffer pain when they are in hell.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Guard yourself as carefully as a fortress with defenses inside and out. Do not let any moment slip away, because those who miss the right moment will experience pain in a state of torment.
rows.riskNotes: The warning of hell and suffering for missed moments may deepen despair, self-blame, or panic in someone already feeling fragile or suicidal.

rows.text: They who are ashamed of what they ought not to be ashamed of, and are not ashamed of what they ought to be ashamed of, such men, embracing false doctrines enter the evil path.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who feel shame about things that aren't shameful, and feel no shame about things that are, end up adopting wrong views and heading down a harmful path.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may intensify feelings of shame and self-blame in someone already struggling with misplaced guilt or moral confusion, as it warns of an evil path for those who misdirect their shame.

rows.text: They who fear when they ought not to fear, and fear not when they ought to fear, such men, embracing false doctrines, enter the evil path.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who are afraid of things that aren't dangerous and not afraid of things that are, end up holding wrong beliefs and heading down a harmful path.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may intensify shame, self-blame, or despair by framing misplaced fear as a sign of false doctrine leading to an evil path, which could be harmful for those already in crisis.

rows.text: They who forbid when there is nothing to be forbidden, and forbid not when there is something to be forbidden, such men, embracing false doctrines, enter the evil path.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who prohibit things that should not be prohibited and fail to prohibit things that should be, hold wrong views and end up on a harmful path.
rows.riskNotes: The condemnation of wrong views and the warning of an evil path may deepen shame, self-blame, or despair in someone already feeling morally flawed or hopeless.

rows.text: They who know what is forbidden as forbidden, and what is not forbidden as not forbidden, such men, embracing the true doctrine, enter the good path.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Those who correctly distinguish between what is off-limits and what is permissible, and who live by the genuine teaching, find the right way.

rows.text: Silently shall I endure abuse as the elephant in battle endures the arrow sent from the bow: for the world is ill-natured.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: I will endure harsh treatment without reacting, just as a war elephant withstands arrows, because people are often unkind.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may encourage someone who has experienced abuse to silently endure further harm, potentially invalidating their need for safety and support.

rows.text: They lead a tamed elephant to battle, the king mounts a tamed elephant; the tamed is the best among men, he who silently endures abuse.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A trained elephant is useful in battle and for a king to ride. In the same way, a person who has learned to stay silent when insulted is the most excellent among people.
rows.riskNotes: May harm those who have experienced abuse by implying that silent endurance is the ideal response, potentially discouraging disclosure or validating mistreatment.

rows.text: Mules are good, if tamed, and noble Sindhu horses, and elephants with large tusks; but he who tames himself is better still.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Tamed animals like mules, thoroughbred horses, and large-tusked elephants are prized, but a person who has disciplined themselves is far more valuable.

rows.text: For with these animals does no man reach the untrodden country (Nirvana), where a tamed man goes on a tamed animal, viz. on his own well-tamed self.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: No one reaches the highest spiritual goal by depending on external things like animals. Only a person who has tamed their own mind and character can get there, using their own self-discipline as the vehicle.

rows.text: The elephant called Dhanapalaka, his temples running with sap, and difficult to hold, does not eat a morsel when bound; the elephant longs for the elephant grove.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A mighty elephant, restrained and miserable, refuses to eat because it yearns for the freedom of its forest home.

rows.text: If a man becomes fat and a great eater, if he is sleepy and rolls himself about, that fool, like a hog fed on wash, is born again and again.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who indulges in overeating, laziness, and excessive sleep is compared to a pig fed on swill; such a foolish person is subject to repeated rebirth.
rows.riskNotes: This passage harshly condemns laziness and gluttony, comparing a person to a hog, which could deeply wound someone already struggling with shame, self-blame, or feelings of worthlessness.

rows.text: This mind of mine went formerly wandering about as it liked, as it listed, as it pleased; but I shall now hold it in thoroughly, as the rider who holds the hook holds in the furious elephant.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The speaker's mind once roamed freely, but now they intend to discipline it tightly, just as a trainer uses a hook to control a fierce elephant.

rows.text: Be not thoughtless, watch your thoughts! Draw yourself out of the evil way, like an elephant sunk in mud.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Don't be careless; pay attention to your own mind. Pull yourself out of harmful patterns, just as an elephant stuck in mud struggles to free itself.
rows.riskNotes: The exhortation to draw oneself out of an 'evil way' may intensify feelings of shame, self-blame, or hopelessness in someone already struggling with those states.

rows.text: If a man find a prudent companion who walks with him, is wise, and lives soberly, he may walk with him, overcoming all dangers, happy, but considerate.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who finds a sensible, wise, and disciplined companion should stay with that companion. Together they can overcome dangers and live happily while remaining considerate.

rows.text: If a man find no prudent companion who walks with him, is wise, and lives soberly, let him walk alone, like a king who has left his conquered country behind,—like an elephant in the forest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When no wise and disciplined companion is available, it is better to travel alone, like a king who has left his realm behind or an elephant in the wild.

rows.text: It is better to live alone, there is no companionship with a fool; let a man walk alone, let him commit no sin, with few wishes, like an elephant in the forest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Living alone is preferable to keeping company with a foolish person. One should live independently, avoid harmful actions, and be content with little, like an elephant wandering freely in the forest.
rows.riskNotes: A person already feeling like a fool or unworthy may interpret this as confirmation that they deserve isolation, deepening shame and self-blame.

rows.text: If an occasion arises, friends are pleasant; enjoyment is pleasant, whatever be the cause; a good work is pleasant in the hour of death; the giving up of all grief is pleasant.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Friends are a comfort when you need them; pleasure is enjoyable no matter its origin; a life of good actions brings ease at death; and releasing all sorrow is the greatest peace.

rows.text: Pleasant in the world is the state of a mother, pleasant the state of a father, pleasant the state of a Samana, pleasant the state of a Brahmana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Being a mother or father is a pleasant experience in life, and so is being a wandering ascetic or a holy person.

rows.text: Pleasant is virtue lasting to old age, pleasant is a faith firmly rooted; pleasant is attainment of intelligence, pleasant is avoiding of sins.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Living with integrity into old age feels good; having steady confidence in what you believe feels good; gaining understanding feels good; staying away from harmful actions feels good.

rows.text: The thirst of a thoughtless man grows like a creeper; he runs from life to life, like a monkey seeking fruit in the forest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person lacks mindfulness, their desires grow and entangle them, driving them to chase fleeting pleasures from one existence to the next, like a monkey leaping after fruit in the trees.

rows.text: Whomsoever this fierce thirst overcomes, full of poison, in this world, his sufferings increase like the abounding Birana grass.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person is overcome by intense, toxic craving, their pain grows rapidly, like a fast-spreading weed.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may intensify feelings of self-blame and hopelessness in someone already struggling with shame or despair, as it attributes increased suffering to one's own craving.

rows.text: He who overcomes this fierce thirst, difficult to be conquered in this world, sufferings fall off from him, like water-drops from a lotus leaf.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person manages to overcome the powerful force of craving, their suffering slips away effortlessly, like water sliding off a lotus leaf.

rows.text: This salutary word I tell you, `Do ye, as many as are here assembled, dig up the root of thirst, as he who wants the sweet-scented Usira root must dig up the Birana grass, that Mara (the tempter) may not crush you again and again, as the stream crushes the reeds.'
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A helpful instruction: dig out the root of craving entirely, like someone who wants a sweet-smelling root must dig up the grass around it, so that temptation doesn't keep breaking you down like a current snapping reeds.
rows.riskNotes: The vivid imagery of being crushed repeatedly may intensify feelings of hopelessness or panic in someone already in despair or suicidal crisis.

rows.text: As a tree, even though it has been cut down, is firm so long as its root is safe, and grows again, thus, unless the feeders of thirst are destroyed, the pain (of life) will return again and again.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Even if a tree is cut down, it will grow back as long as its root is intact. Similarly, unless the underlying causes of craving are eliminated, suffering will keep recurring.
rows.riskNotes: The statement that pain will return again and again may deepen hopelessness for someone already in despair, even though the passage offers a path to cessation.

rows.text: He whose thirst running towards pleasure is exceeding strong in the thirty-six channels, the waves will carry away that misguided man, viz. his desires which are set on passion.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person whose intense craving for pleasure flows through many channels gets swept away by their own desires, like waves carrying off someone who is lost.
rows.riskNotes: Calling a person 'misguided' and warning of being carried away by desires may intensify feelings of shame and self-blame in someone already struggling with those states.

rows.text: The channels run everywhere, the creeper (of passion) stands sprouting; if you see the creeper springing up, cut its root by means of knowledge.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Desires spread everywhere like vines; when you notice a new desire emerging, use clear understanding to eliminate it at its source.

rows.text: A creature's pleasures are extravagant and luxurious; sunk in lust and looking for pleasure, men undergo (again and again) birth and decay.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People chase extravagant pleasures and get lost in desire, which keeps them trapped in cycles of suffering and rebirth.

rows.text: Men, driven on by thirst, run about like a snared hare; held in fetters and bonds, they undergo pain for a long time, again and again.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When craving drives a person, they become like a hare caught in a snare, trapped and suffering over and over.
rows.riskNotes: The imagery of being trapped and undergoing repeated pain may deepen hopelessness in someone already feeling despair or suicidal.

rows.text: Men, driven on by thirst, run about like a snared hare; let therefore the mendicant drive out thirst, by striving after passionlessness for himself.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People are driven by craving and run around like a trapped rabbit. Therefore, a monk should eliminate craving by working toward dispassion for himself.
rows.riskNotes: The imagery of being snared by one's own thirst may intensify self-blame and shame in someone already feeling at fault for their suffering.

rows.text: He who having got rid of the forest (of lust) (i.e. after having reached Nirvana) gives himself over to forest-life (i.e. to lust), and who, when removed from the forest (i.e. from lust), runs to the forest (i.e. to lust), look at that man! though free, he runs into bondage.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has freed themselves from craving but then deliberately returns to it, and when pulled away, runs back again, is like someone who escapes bondage only to choose it anew.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's depiction of returning to bondage after freedom may intensify self-blame and hopelessness in someone struggling with relapse or addiction.

rows.text: Wise people do not call that a strong fetter which is made of iron, wood, or hemp; far stronger is the care for precious stones and rings, for sons and a wife.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Physical chains are not the strongest bonds; emotional investment in possessions and family ties holds people far more tightly.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel dismissive or hurtful to someone grieving the loss of a spouse or child, as it frames familial love as a fetter.

rows.text: That fetter wise people call strong which drags down, yields, but is difficult to undo; after having cut this at last, people leave the world, free from cares, and leaving desires and pleasures behind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A strong fetter that drags one down and is difficult to break is recognized by the wise; once it is cut, a person becomes free from worry and leaves behind desires and pleasures.

rows.text: Those who are slaves to passions, run down with the stream (of desires), as a spider runs down the web which he has made himself; when they have cut this, at last, wise people leave the world free from cares, leaving all affection behind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who are controlled by their cravings get carried along by desires, like a spider moving along its own web. Those with insight cut through this web, letting go of all attachments, and live without mental burdens.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of being a 'slave to passions' may intensify self-blame and shame, while the call to leave affection behind could feel invalidating to those grieving or in relational pain, and the phrase 'leave the world' might be dangerously misread by someone with suicidal ideation.

rows.text: Give up what is before, give up what is behind, give up what is in the middle, when thou goest to the other shore of existence; if thy mind is altogether free, thou wilt not again enter into birth and decay.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Let go of clinging to past, future, and present experiences. When the mind is fully liberated, one crosses beyond existence and is no longer subject to birth and decay.
rows.riskNotes: The call to give up all attachments, including to past and present, could be misinterpreted as encouraging detachment from life itself, which may be harmful for those in despair, grieving, or experiencing suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: If a man is tossed about by doubts, full of strong passions, and yearning only for what is delightful, his thirst will grow more and more, and he will indeed make his fetters strong.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is uncertain, driven by intense desires, and always chasing pleasure will only increase their cravings and tighten their own bondage.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may intensify feelings of self-blame or shame in someone already struggling with their own perceived flaws, as it attributes bondage to one's own doubts and passions.

rows.text: If a man delights in quieting doubts, and, always reflecting, dwells on what is not delightful (the impurity of the body, &c.), he certainly will remove, nay, he will cut the fetter of Mara.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who enjoys clearing up doubts and keeps reflecting on unpleasant truths (like the body's unattractive nature) will break free from the grip of craving and delusion.
rows.riskNotes: Focusing on the body's impurity may intensify feelings of shame or self-blame in someone already struggling with those states.

rows.text: He who has reached the consummation, who does not tremble, who is without thirst and without sin, he has broken all the thorns of life: this will be his last body.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has attained the highest spiritual goal is free from craving and wrongdoing, remains unshaken, and has overcome all life's difficulties. They will not be reborn again.

rows.text: He who is without thirst and without affection, who understands the words and their interpretation, who knows the order of letters (those which are before and which are after), he has received his last body, he is called the great sage, the great man.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is free from craving and attachment, who fully understands language and its structure, has reached their final birth and is considered a great sage.

rows.text: `I have conquered all, I know all, in all conditions of life I am free from taint; I have left all, and through the destruction of thirst I am free; having learnt myself, whom shall I teach?'
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The speaker claims to have overcome all obstacles, to possess complete understanding, and to remain untainted by any circumstance. By letting go of everything and extinguishing craving, they have attained liberation. Having realized the truth through their own insight, they question the need to teach anyone else.

rows.text: The gift of the law exceeds all gifts; the sweetness of the law exceeds all sweetness; the delight in the law exceeds all delights; the extinction of thirst overcomes all pain.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The teaching of the Dhamma is the greatest gift, the sweetest thing, and the highest delight; the ending of craving eliminates all suffering.

rows.text: Pleasures destroy the foolish, if they look not for the other shore; the foolish by his thirst for pleasures destroys himself, as if he were his own enemy.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who chase pleasures without aiming for liberation end up destroying themselves, acting as their own enemy.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's message that one destroys oneself like an enemy may intensify self-blame, shame, and despair, and could be harmful for someone experiencing suicidal ideation.

rows.text: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by passion: therefore a gift bestowed on the passionless brings great reward.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as weeds ruin farmland, strong desires ruin people. Giving to those who have overcome desire yields great benefit.

rows.text: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by hatred: therefore a gift bestowed on those who do not hate brings great reward.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Hatred harms a person the way weeds harm a field. A gift given to someone who is free from hatred brings a large benefit.

rows.text: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by vanity: therefore a gift bestowed on those who are free from vanity brings great reward.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Weeds ruin fields, and vanity ruins people. Giving to those who are not vain is especially beneficial.

rows.text: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by lust: therefore a gift bestowed on those who are free from lust brings great reward.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as weeds damage fields, lust damages human beings. Therefore, a gift given to those who are free from lust brings a great reward.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may intensify shame or self-blame in someone struggling with lust or desire, as it frames lust as a damaging force.

rows.text: Restraint in the eye is good, good is restraint in the ear, in the nose restraint is good, good is restraint in the tongue.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Exercising control over one's senses—sight, hearing, smell, and speech—is beneficial.

rows.text: In the body restraint is good, good is restraint in speech, in thought restraint is good, good is restraint in all things. A Bhikshu, restrained in all things, is freed from all pain.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Restraint in physical actions, words, and thoughts is beneficial. A person who practices restraint in all areas becomes free from suffering.

rows.text: He who controls his hand, he who controls his feet, he who controls his speech, he who is well controlled, he who delights inwardly, who is collected, who is solitary and content, him they call Bhikshu.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who controls their physical actions, speech, and mind, who takes pleasure in inner stillness, remains composed, and is content with solitude, is called a true mendicant.

rows.text: The Bhikshu who controls his mouth, who speaks wisely and calmly, who teaches the meaning and the law, his word is sweet.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who guards their speech, speaks with insight and composure, and teaches what is meaningful and correct, has words that are pleasing to hear.

rows.text: He who dwells in the law, delights in the law, meditates on the law, follows the law, that Bhikshu will never fall away from the true law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who lives in accordance with the teachings, takes joy in them, meditates on them, and follows them will never stray from the true path.

rows.text: Let him not despise what he has received, nor ever envy others: a mendicant who envies others does not obtain peace of mind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A mendicant should appreciate what they receive without belittling it or envying others. Envy disrupts mental peace.

rows.text: A Bhikshu who, though he receives little, does not despise what he has received, even the gods will praise him, if his life is pure, and if he is not slothful.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A monk who does not look down on the small amount he receives, who lives purely and is not idle, earns praise even from divine beings.

rows.text: He who never identifies himself with name and form, and does not grieve over what is no more, he indeed is called a Bhikshu.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who does not cling to a fixed identity or material things, and who does not mourn what has passed, is truly a renunciant.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel dismissive to someone in fresh grief, as it suggests that not grieving is a spiritual ideal.
