rows.text: The Bhikshu who acts with kindness, who is calm in the doctrine of Buddha, will reach the quiet place (Nirvana), cessation of natural desires, and happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A monastic who treats others with kindness and stays composed in following the Buddha's path will reach a state of stillness, the end of compulsive desires, and lasting well-being.

rows.text: O Bhikshu, empty this boat! if emptied, it will go quickly; having cut off passion and hatred thou wilt go to Nirvana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A boat moves quickly when bailed out; similarly, one reaches liberation by cutting off craving and hatred.
rows.riskNotes: The command to cut off passion and hatred may intensify feelings of shame or self-blame in someone already struggling with these emotions, as it can be heard as a demand to suppress natural feelings.

rows.text: Cut off the five (senses), leave the five, rise above the five. A Bhikshu, who has escaped from the five fetters, he is called Oghatinna, `saved from the flood.'
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Letting go of the five aggregates, rising above them, and breaking free from the five fetters is what it means to cross the flood of suffering.

rows.text: Meditate, O Bhikshu, and be not heedless! Do not direct thy thought to what gives pleasure that thou mayest not for thy heedlessness have to swallow the iron ball (in hell), and that thou mayest not cry out when burning, `This is pain.'
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A mendicant is told to meditate and avoid careless thoughts of pleasure, because heedlessness leads to intense suffering.
rows.riskNotes: The vivid threat of hellish torment for heedlessness could intensify self-blame, shame, panic, despair, or suicidal thoughts in a fragile person.

rows.text: Without knowledge there is no meditation, without meditation there is no knowledge: he who has knowledge and meditation is near unto Nirvana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Understanding and meditation depend on each other. A person who develops both is close to liberation.

rows.text: A Bhikshu who has entered his empty house, and whose mind is tranquil, feels a more than human delight when he sees the law clearly.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A monk who has gone to a quiet, empty dwelling and calmed his mind experiences an extraordinary joy when he directly understands the truth.

rows.text: As soon as he has considered the origin and destruction of the elements (khandha) of the body, he finds happiness and joy which belong to those who know the immortal (Nirvana).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person reflects on how the physical components of the body arise and pass away, they experience the happiness and joy that come with understanding the deathless state.
rows.riskNotes: The direct reference to the destruction of the body's elements may distress someone in fresh grief or reinforce suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: And this is the beginning here for a wise Bhikshu: watchfulness over the senses, contentedness, restraint under the law; keep noble friends whose life is pure, and who are not slothful.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A wise person starts by guarding their senses, being content, following ethical discipline, and keeping company with diligent, virtuous friends.

rows.text: Let him live in charity, let him be perfect in his duties; then in the fulness of delight he will make an end of suffering.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should be generous and fulfill their responsibilities well; then, with deep joy, they will overcome suffering.
rows.riskNotes: The call to be 'perfect in duties' may intensify shame and self-blame in those already feeling inadequate, and the phrase 'make an end of suffering' could be misread by someone with suicidal ideation.

rows.text: As the Vassika plant sheds its withered flowers, men should shed passion and hatred, O ye Bhikshus!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Like a plant that naturally sheds its withered blossoms, one should release intense craving and hostility.
rows.riskNotes: Urging the shedding of hatred may feel dismissive to someone currently processing justified anger toward an aggressor or relational harm.

rows.text: The Bhikshu whose body and tongue and mind are quieted, who is collected, and has rejected the baits of the world, he is called quiet.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A monk who has calmed his actions, speech, and thoughts, who is focused and has let go of worldly temptations, is truly at peace.

rows.text: Rouse thyself by thyself, examine thyself by thyself, thus self-protected and attentive wilt thou live happily, O Bhikshu!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should rely on their own effort to wake up and investigate themselves. Through self-protection and constant awareness, they can achieve a happy life.
rows.riskNotes: The call to examine oneself may intensify self-criticism for someone already experiencing acute shame or self-blame.

rows.text: For self is the lord of self, self is the refuge of self; therefore curb thyself as the merchant curbs a good horse.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person is their own master and their own source of safety, so they should discipline themselves as carefully as a merchant trains a good horse.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on self as sole refuge may intensify feelings of personal failure for someone already struggling with shame or self-blame.

rows.text: The Bhikshu, full of delight, who is calm in the doctrine of Buddha will reach the quiet place (Nirvana), cessation of natural desires, and happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A monk who finds joy and calm in the Buddha's teaching attains the peaceful state of Nirvana, where desires cease and happiness is found.
rows.riskNotes: The mention of 'cessation' and a 'quiet place' could be misread as an endorsement of death by someone in despair or with suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: He who, even as a young Bhikshu, applies himself to the doctrine of Buddha, brightens up this world, like the moon when free from clouds.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A young monk who earnestly follows the Buddha's teachings brightens the world, just as the moon shines when unobscured by clouds.

rows.text: Stop the stream valiantly, drive away the desires, O Brahmana! When you have understood the destruction of all that was made, you will understand that which was not made.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Make a determined effort to halt the current of craving and let go of desires. Once you see clearly how all conditioned things fall apart, you will know the unconditioned.

rows.text: If the Brahmana has reached the other shore in both laws (in restraint and contemplation), all bonds vanish from him who has obtained knowledge.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When someone has fully developed both self-discipline and meditative concentration, all inner bonds are broken through the realization of wisdom.

rows.text: He for whom there is neither this nor that shore, nor both, him, the fearless and unshackled, I call indeed a Brahmana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has gone beyond all dualities and attachments, free from fear and all bonds, is a true spiritual adept.

rows.text: He who is thoughtful, blameless, settled, dutiful, without passions, and who has attained the highest end, him I call indeed a Brahmana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is attentive, morally upright, calm, responsible, free from craving, and has reached the ultimate goal is truly a noble one.

rows.text: The sun is bright by day, the moon shines by night, the warrior is bright in his armour, the Brahmana is bright in his meditation; but Buddha, the Awakened, is bright with splendour day and night.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Different things shine in different conditions: the sun by day, the moon by night, a warrior in battle gear, a meditator in deep focus. But a fully awakened person radiates continuously, regardless of circumstances.

rows.text: Because a man is rid of evil, therefore he is called Brahmana; because he walks quietly, therefore he is called Samana; because he has sent away his own impurities, therefore he is called Pravragita (Pabbagita, a pilgrim).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person is called a Brahmana for having removed evil, a Samana for living in a calm manner, and a pilgrim for having abandoned inner impurities.

rows.text: No one should attack a Brahmana, but no Brahmana (if attacked) should let himself fly at his aggressor! Woe to him who strikes a Brahmana, more woe to him who flies at his aggressor!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: No one should harm a holy person, but if a holy person is attacked, they should not retaliate. It is bad to strike such a person, but worse for that person to strike back.
rows.riskNotes: The warning of 'more woe' for retaliating may intensify self-blame and shame in those who have reacted to harm, and could feel invalidating to victims of relational harm or those processing rage.

rows.text: It advantages a Brahmana not a little if he holds his mind back from the pleasures of life; when all wish to injure has vanished, pain will cease.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A holy person gains great benefit by restraining the mind from sensual pleasures; when all ill-will is gone, suffering ends.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who does not offend by body, word, or thought, and is controlled on these three points.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A truly noble person is one who avoids causing harm through actions, words, or thoughts, and has disciplined these three areas.

rows.text: After a man has once understood the law as taught by the Well-awakened (Buddha), let him worship it carefully, as the Brahmana worships the sacrificial fire.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Understanding the Buddha's teaching calls for a response of devoted care, like the way a Brahmin tends a sacred fire.

rows.text: A man does not become a Brahmana by his platted hair, by his family, or by birth; in whom there is truth and righteousness, he is blessed, he is a Brahmana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person's true worth is not determined by their hairstyle, family, or birth, but by whether they live with honesty and moral integrity.

rows.text: What is the use of platted hair, O fool! what of the raiment of goat-skins? Within thee there is ravening, but the outside thou makest clean.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Outward religious symbols like matted hair or animal-skin clothing are meaningless if a person is consumed by inner greed. They only clean the surface while the inside remains corrupt.
rows.riskNotes: This harsh rebuke could deepen feelings of worthlessness and self-hatred in someone already struggling with shame or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: The man who wears dirty raiments, who is emaciated and covered with veins, who lives alone in the forest, and meditates, him I call indeed a Brahmana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who dresses in worn-out clothing, has a lean body with visible veins, stays by themselves in the forest, and devotes themselves to meditation is what I call a genuine spiritual practitioner.
rows.riskNotes: The description of an emaciated, solitary ascetic as an ideal could be harmful to those struggling with self-blame, despair, or suicidal thoughts, as it may glorify self-neglect and suffering.

rows.text: I do not call a man a Brahmana because of his origin or of his mother. He is indeed arrogant, and he is wealthy: but the poor, who is free from all attachments, him I call indeed a Brahmana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person's true worth does not come from birth or wealth. Someone arrogant and rich is not noble; the one who is poor but has let go of all clinging is truly noble.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has cut all fetters, who never trembles, is independent and unshackled.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has broken all inner bonds, who is fearless, self-reliant, and completely free.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has cut the strap and the thong, the chain with all that pertains to it, who has burst the bar, and is awakened.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has cut off all forms of inner bondage—like straps, cords, chains, and bars—and has become fully awake is truly noble.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, though he has committed no offence, endures reproach, bonds, and stripes, who has endurance for his force, and strength for his army.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A truly noble person is one who, though innocent, patiently endures blame, imprisonment, and physical punishment, relying on endurance as their power and inner strength as their protection.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may be harmful to victims of relational harm or those angry at an aggressor, as it could be perceived as glorifying passive endurance of unjust suffering.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is free from anger, dutiful, virtuous, without appetite, who is subdued, and has received his last body.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The ideal person is someone who doesn't get angry, acts with integrity, has no addictive desires, is in control of themselves, and has reached the end of the cycle of rebirth.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who does not cling to pleasures, like water on a lotus leaf, like a mustard seed on the point of a needle.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who doesn't hold onto pleasures, like water that slides off a lotus leaf or a mustard seed that won't stay on a needle point, is truly noble.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, even here, knows the end of his suffering, has put down his burden, and is unshackled.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has directly understood the end of suffering, has laid down their heavy load, and is completely free from all bonds is truly noble.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana whose knowledge is deep, who possesses wisdom, who knows the right way and the wrong, and has attained the highest end.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person of deep understanding and wisdom, who clearly distinguishes between right and wrong and has reached the ultimate spiritual goal, is truly noble.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who keeps aloof both from laymen and from mendicants, who frequents no houses, and has but few desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A genuine spiritual person stays detached from both ordinary people and fellow monks, avoids visiting homes, and lives with very few wants.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's praise of aloofness and few desires may deepen isolation and feelings of inadequacy in those already struggling with shame, despair, or relational harm.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who finds no fault with other beings, whether feeble or strong, and does not kill nor cause slaughter.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A truly noble person does not criticize any living being, whether weak or strong, and refrains from killing or causing others to kill.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is tolerant with the intolerant, mild with fault-finders, and free from passion among the passionate.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who remains patient and gentle even when others are hostile or critical, and who does not get caught up in the desires and emotions that drive others.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel invalidating to those who are angry at an aggressor or have experienced relational harm, as it praises tolerance and mildness toward difficult people.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana from whom anger and hatred, pride and envy have dropt like a mustard seed from the point of a needle.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has shed anger, hatred, pride, and envy so completely that they fall away like a tiny seed from a needle's tip is truly noble.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who utters true speech, instructive and free from harshness, so that he offend no one.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person of genuine integrity uses words that are honest, helpful, and gentle, never causing offense.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who takes nothing in the world that is not given him, be it long or short, small or large, good or bad.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true holy person does not take anything that hasn't been freely offered, no matter its size or value.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who fosters no desires for this world or for the next, has no inclinations, and is unshackled.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A truly noble person is one who has let go of all wanting, both for this life and any afterlife, and who lives without clinging or being bound by anything.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has no interests, and when he has understood (the truth), does not say How, how? and who has reached the depth of the Immortal.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true Brahmana is free from worldly attachments, has realized the truth without lingering doubts, and has attained the deathless state.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who in this world is above good and evil, above the bondage of both, free from grief from sin, and from impurity.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has transcended attachment to both good and evil, and is free from sorrow, guilt, and inner impurity, is considered truly noble.
rows.riskNotes: The phrase 'free from grief' may feel invalidating to someone experiencing fresh grief, as if their sorrow is a state to be transcended rather than honored.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is bright like the moon, pure, serene, undisturbed, and in whom all gaiety is extinct.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is calm, clear, and untroubled, having let go of all excitement and frivolous joy.
rows.riskNotes: The phrase 'all gaiety is extinct' could be misinterpreted as endorsing emotional numbness, potentially deepening feelings of despair or hopelessness in someone already struggling with joylessness.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has traversed this miry road, the impassable world and its vanity, who has gone through, and reached the other shore, is thoughtful, guileless, free from doubts, free from attachment, and content.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: This describes a person who has made it through the difficult, deceptive world, reached a stable state beyond it, and lives with clear awareness, honesty, no uncertainty, no clinging, and deep satisfaction.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who in this world, leaving all desires, travels about without a home, and in whom all concupiscence is extinct.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has given up all desires, lives without a fixed home, and has completely ended lust is truly noble.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, leaving all longings, travels about without a home, and in whom all covetousness is extinct.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true spiritual person has given up all desires, lives without a fixed home, and has completely ended greed.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, after leaving all bondage to men, has risen above all bondage to the gods, and is free from all and every bondage.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A truly noble person is one who has let go of all dependencies on other people and even on divine beings, and is completely free from every kind of bondage.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has left what gives pleasure and what gives pain, who is cold, and free from all germs (of renewed life), the hero who has conquered all the worlds.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has gone beyond both enjoyment and suffering, who is detached and free from the causes of rebirth, and who has overcome all realms of existence.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who knows the destruction and the return of beings everywhere, who is free from bondage, welfaring (Sugata), and awakened (Buddha).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who understands the arising and ceasing of all beings everywhere, who is free from bondage, well-gone, and awakened, is a genuine holy person.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana whose path the gods do not know, nor spirits (Gandharvas), nor men, whose passions are extinct, and who is an Arhat (venerable).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage describes a person whose spiritual attainment is so complete that even gods and spirits cannot grasp it, and who has extinguished all inner passions.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who calls nothing his own, whether it be before, behind, or between, who is poor, and free from the love of the world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has no sense of ownership over past, present, or future, who lives without possessions and is free from worldly desire, is truly noble.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana, the manly, the noble, the hero, the great sage, the conqueror, the impassible, the accomplished, the awakened.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A truly noble person is one who has mastered themselves, remains unshaken, and has reached full understanding and awakening.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who knows his former abodes, who sees heaven and hell, has reached the end of births, is perfect in knowledge, a sage, and whose perfections are all perfect.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who remembers past lives, understands the full range of existence, has ended the cycle of rebirth, and has perfected all spiritual qualities is truly awakened.
